- Bimagrumab Decoded: The Muscle-Building Drug That Could Fix Ozempic Muscle Loss (2026 Update): Kind: captions Language: en What if there was a drug that could help you lose fat while actually building more muscle at the same time? No steroids, no magic pills, just a targeted antibbody that's already in clinical trials showing 3 to 7% muscle gain and double-digit fat loss. And it might solve one of the biggest problems with osmpic and zepbound losing precious muscle along with the weight. Today from longevica labs bimma groomab decoded the experimental muscle preserving drug that could change longevity and smarter weight loss. This is educational only. Bimagrumab is investigational not approved anywhere. Always consult your doctor. Do not act on this information. Bimmagroomab is a monoconal antibbody originally from Novartis, now owned by Eli Lily through the Versanis acquisition. It specifically targets Activin type 2 receptors, blocking two key proteins, myostatin and actin A. Myostatin is the body's natural break on muscle growth. When you block it, muscles can grow bigger and stronger. At the same time, it reduces fat mass, especially the dangerous visceral kind. As of January 2026, it's in phase two trials. Not approved, still experimental. BIMagrumab interrupts the signals that normally limit muscle size. By blocking those receptors, it promotes true muscle hypertrophy, actual increase in muscle fiber size, not just water or temporary pump. It also shifts metabolism to burn more fat, particularly around organs. Unlike anabolic steroids, it uses a completely different pathway, no hormonal disruption. And unlike GLP1 drugs alone, it actively protects or even increases lean mass during fat loss. In phase two studies for obesity and sarcopenia, participants gained about 3 to 7% lean muscle mass while losing 10 to 21% body fat. In the 2024 Believe trial, combining it with sagutide, people lost more fat than with sagotide alone and kept or even added lean mass. That's huge because normally GLP-1 drugs cause 20 to 40% of weight loss to come from muscle. Functional improvements, strength, walking speed were mixed. Some people got stronger, others didn't see big daily life gains. Right now, the main trial is phase two combining biagrammab with tzepide zbound in people with obesity but no diabetes. estimated primary results around April 2026. An earlier diabetes combo trial was stopped, not for safety, but for strategic reasons. Lily is clearly focusing on GLP-1 combinations. Even if successful, approval is likely still several years away. Muscle loss, sarcopenia, is one of the fastest ways aging accelerates frailty and metabolic decline. Higher muscle mass correlates with better insulin sensitivity, lower inflammation, reduced fall risk, and longer health span. The McGroomab could help maintain or rebuild muscle during calorie restriction, illness, or just normal aging. If proven safe long term, it might become a powerful tool for healthy aging. GLP1 drugs like Ompic, WGOI, and Zetbound are incredible for fat loss, but they often strip away muscle too. Sometimes 20 to 40% of total weight lost that leads to skinny fat, slower metabolism, and weakness. Beagrammab changes the equation. Trials show it protects muscle during aggressive fat loss. Future potential a combo therapy GLP-1 for appetite/fatburn plus bagrammab for muscle preservation in trials so far beagrammab has been generally well tolerated. Most common side effects: muscle spasms or cramps 10 to 15%. Diarrhea, acne, joint pain, mild injection site reactions. No major organ toxicity or serious safety signals reported, but long-term safety is still unknown. Trials are relatively short. Not everyone qualifies. Strict exclusion criteria apply. If approved, the biggest winners could be older adults fighting sarcopenia, people on GLP-1 drugs losing too much muscle, those with sarcopenic obesity, high fat plus low muscle, and patients with metabolic syndrome or insulin resistance. It's not a standalone miracle. lifestyle resistance training plus protein will still be essential. It's still experimental. No approval date in sight. Muscle mass gains don't always translate to huge strength or mobility improvements. It's an injection likely expensive as a monoconal antibbody. Long-term effects on tendons, heart, muscle quality, unknown. Will insurance cover it only for specific diagnosis? Many questions remain. Bimmagab is one of the most exciting muscle preserving drugs in development right now. It could transform how we approach weight loss and healthy aging, keeping muscle while losing fat, potentially extending health span. But it's months away. Trials are ongoing. For now, prioritize proven basics. Lift weights, eat enough protein, manage calories wisely. Longevica Labs will keep following this science. Subscribe for updates when those 2026 results drop. Thanks for watching. Consult your doctor on anything

- Forget Ozempic? This New Drug is 3x Stronger: Kind: captions Language: en Hey, imagine sitting at the table looking at your favorite food and feeling really hungry and suddenly you just don't feel like eating as much anymore. That's exactly what happened to many people in the 2026 clinical trials. One new medicine helped them lose an average of 24 to 28% of their body weight in 48 to 68 weeks. This medicine is called retatrutide. It's still in the final testing phase. In this video, I'm going to explain in a simple way how it works on your hunger, your gut, your brain, and your metabolism only using the proven facts. Stay with me because this could really change how you think about losing weight. What is retatrutide? Comparison with Ozempic. Let's start with something you probably already know, Ozempic or Wegovy. Those medicines mainly work with one hormone that reduces your hunger. Retatrutide does the same but stronger because it copies three natural hormones that your body already makes. It's like Ozempic with extra help. It reduces hunger, helps your body handle food better, and makes you burn more calories even when you're sitting or sleeping. Retatrutide is a once-a-week injection. It's not yet approved for everyone, but the big studies have already shown very strong results. How does it work? Simple explanation. So, how does it actually work? I'll explain it in a very simple way. The medicine sends three signals at the same time. One signal tells your stomach empty slower so you feel full faster. Another helps keep your blood sugar steady, and the third puts your body in a mild burn mode so it uses stored fat for energy more easily. Because of this, many people feel calmer about food all day. They eat smaller portions, think less about snacks, and feel full sooner. In the studies, participants said their brain finally got a calm message saying, "You've had enough." instead of the usual strong urge to keep eating. Effect on your gut. Let's talk about your gut for a moment. Your gut has trillions of tiny bacteria that help you digest food and stay healthy. Does retatrutide change them? Honestly, there are still no specific human studies on retatrutide and gut bacteria, but with similar medicines, we often see that the good bacteria increase and the mix becomes healthier. This may support your metabolism. Scientists are doing more research on this right now, and I'll keep you updated when new information comes out. Effect on brain and metabolism. Retatrutide also works in your brain so you don't get that annoying rebound hunger you sometimes feel when you only eat less. At the same time, it helps your body burn more calories even while it rests and breaks down fat in your liver faster. The studies measured this and saw real results. People in the trials also noticed great improvements. Their blood sugar went down nicely, liver fat improved, and in one study, people with knee pain lost 28.7% of their body weight in 68 weeks and could move much easier with less pain. Many said they simply felt more energetic every day. Of course, I want to be completely honest with you about both the good and the not-so-good sides. The proven benefits are impressive. On average, people lost about 24% of their weight after 48 weeks and up to 28.7% after 68 weeks. Many reached 15 or even 20% weight loss, numbers that can really improve your health and daily life. The weight kept dropping without a big plateau. A lot of people also reported better blood sugar, easier movement, and more energy. But, there are side effects, too. The most common ones happen especially in the beginning when the dose increases: nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, or constipation. For most people, these were mild to moderate. Some noticed their heart rate went up a little bit, but it usually came back down after a few months. Around 12 to 18% of people stopped the medicine because of side effects. Like with any big weight loss, you can lose some muscle, but it's similar to other medicines when you eat enough protein and stay active. Long-term safety is still being studied. Common questions. Let's answer a few honest questions that many people are asking. When will retatrutide be available on the market? The big studies continue until 2026. The application to the health authorities will probably be submitted later this year. If everything goes well, experts expect it to become available sometime in 2027 or 2028. So, we still have to wait a bit. How much will it cost? There is no official price yet. Medicines like Ozempic and Mounjaro are already quite expensive. Retatrutide will probably be in the same price range, but we have to wait and see what the company and insurance companies decide. Is it better than Ozempic? From the studies so far, it seems stronger for weight loss, but it also causes more side effects for some people. It's not automatically better for everyone. It depends on your own body and health. Exciting science. The most important thing I want you to remember is this. This is exciting science, but it is not a magic pill. It works best when you combine it with healthy eating, moving your body, and getting good sleep. Always talk to your own doctor first before thinking about any new medicine. They know your personal situation best. What do you think? Does retatrutide sound exciting for the future or do you prefer to stick with the options we already know? Thank you so much for watching with me today. Take care of yourself, and I'll see you in the next one.

- Abnehmen ohne Diät mit Ozempic: Wundermittel oder gefährlich?: Kind: captions Language: en Elon Musk did it, as did Wopi Goldberg, Robbie Williams, and Oprah Winfrey. I'm also talking about Hollywood's latest obsession: an injection that allows you to lose a lot of weight quickly without any exercise or diet. Thanks to social media, an incredible hype has developed around the drug Osempic. Demand is so high that there have been repeated supply shortages worldwide over the last two years. But will Osempic and similar drugs really live up to expectations? Are they truly the long-awaited miracle cure that will turn us all into supermodels? If you've already considered trying Osempic, please watch this video first, because I'll tell you right now: there are several [music] catches. Many doctors I know confirm it: the stream of people wanting to get the new weight-loss injection prescribed by their family doctor shows no sign of abating. On the contrary, the injection isn't actually that new. Osempic has been approved in Europe since 2018, and a similar product has been on the market since 2009. It's actually a diabetes medication, but because it turned out Because osempik helps with weight loss, it has been prescribed more and more often off-label, meaning for a condition for which it is not actually approved. Osempic is the brand name for a specific product from the same manufacturer. Since 2023, there has also been the preparation wegovi, which also contains the active ingredient semaglutite but is intended for overweight people. Meanwhile, there are several similar products from different manufacturers, differing either in the active ingredients or the dosage. They all work by mimicking natural gut hormones, the so-called incretin hormones, especially glucagonic peptide-1. Among other things, they help regulate blood sugar levels. Active ingredients such as semaglutite, dulaglutite, and liraglutite have the same effect. This group of medications is therefore called GLP-1 analogs, GLP-1 receptor agonists, and ingretin mimetics. Why exactly they help with weight loss is not yet fully understood. The current scientific consensus is that they stimulate the hunger center in the brain and delay gastric emptying. This simply leads to eating less. The research on this is clear. The largest study to date, from Great Britain, compared the effects of semaglutite over a period of 68 weeks with a placebo. Both groups received support in the areas of nutrition and fitness. The result: the semaglutite group lost an average of almost 15% of their body weight, while the control group lost only about 2.5%. Interestingly, taking semaglutite seems to have further effects. The Select trial, published at the end of 2023, concluded that GLP-1 analogs significantly reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease and overall mortality. They led to improved blood pressure and blood lipid levels. Benefits for bone health, as well as an antidepressant and neuroprotective effect, are also being discussed. Furthermore, there are indications that semaglutite might also be suitable for treating addiction. It really does sound almost like a magic bullet, or perhaps a miracle cure. However, many of the effects are probably related to weight loss. Obesity increases the risk of diabetes, as well as high blood pressure, fatty liver disease, and strokes. The same area in the brain that controls appetite and the rewarding effect of food is also responsible for addictive behavior. It would therefore make sense if the medication also... It can be effective for addiction, but these effects come at a high price. First, it's not easy to get the medication; it's only covered by health insurance for people with diabetes. VGovi is approved for weight loss above a certain BMI, but because it's considered a purely lifestyle drug, German health insurance companies don't currently reimburse it at all. Anyone who still wants it pays approximately €300 to €400 per month. On top of that, there are delivery problems; the products are often unavailable in pharmacies. Another hurdle is that you have to inject the medication. With semaglute, i.e., with osempik and VGovi, you inject the active ingredient once a week into the subcutaneous fat tissue using a so-called pen. With liraglute, it's even once a day. Anyone willing to do this must be prepared for severe side effects. GLP-1 analogs often lead to gastrointestinal problems such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, or cramps. Users report vomiting several times a day and having no energy to cope with everyday life. The joy of eating often disappears completely. Many osempik users develop a veritable... Aversion to their favorite foods is a recurring issue, and serious long-term side effects are also discussed. Taking the medication could increase the risk of intestinal obstruction, gallbladder problems, inflammation of the pancreas, and thyroid and pancreatic cancer. However, this has not yet been clearly proven in studies. In the USA, a class-action lawsuit is currently underway against two manufacturers. The affected individuals claim that taking Osempik led to gastroparesis, i.e., paralysis of the stomach. This can be life-threatening in some circumstances. In the USA, one can be sued quickly. Whether there is any truth to the allegations remains to be seen, but we must be aware that research on GLP-1 agonists is still in its infancy. They have only recently been tested, especially in non-diabetics. Furthermore, the doses used for weight loss are considerably higher than those used to treat diabetes. We simply don't yet know what the long-term effects on the body will be. This is another crucial point regarding the weight-loss injection: it only works as long as you use it. Most users who discontinue the medication regain their starting weight after a year. So, what is the conclusion? Medications like osempik can certainly be a good choice for people with diabetes, at least if the diabetes is so advanced that it can no longer be reversed through lifestyle changes. The same applies to people suffering from massive obesity, as it is medically proven that obesity increases the risk of many diseases. Wegovi can then be a complement to more exercise and healthy eating. In cases of severe suffering, many sufferers accept the risks of the medication. For those who only want to lose a few extra kilos, GLP-1 analogs are not suitable. The potential side effects are immense, and we still know far too little about what the medications do to us in the long term. Again, resist the temptation to take a shortcut and instead strive for healthier habits. With small daily microsteps, you can achieve more than you think. It's primarily about avoiding the yo-yo effect, and that's why my colleague Dr. Sepid Rawahi and I have developed a weight loss course that achieves sustainable and long-term results. You can find the link above or below in the description. Take a look! Sincerely, Ulich Bauhofer [Music]

The fact that people no longer feel hungry , no longer crave their favorite food, and above all lose weight as a result of taking Osemp, was actually not the reason why this drug was developed. Rather, the pharmaceutical industry was researching a way to treat type 2 diabetes, and they were primarily looking to develop a drug that controls patients' blood sugar levels . And when it was tested on people with type 2 diabetes, it was found that one of the side effects was that these people all simply lost weight. And now, when Osemp is injected, it doesn't work via GLP1, but via semaglutide, which then docks onto the same receptor in the hypothalamus, and because it has such a long half-life, it signals for 7 days, without you eating, that you are full and that you are not hungry . Everything is clear. Is this really the right way to go, taking this lazy path ? Is that healthy? Does that even make sense? To answer that question , we need to look at why people become fat in the first place. The reason people get fat is quite simple: they eat more calories than they burn. But if we look at the genes, we see that it's not quite fair, because there are people who get fat four times faster than others, even though they consume the same amount of excess calories . A person needs approximately 7700 extra calories to build up 1 kg of fat. And if we now consider that each of us eats 100 calories more per day than we need, then the very first people will gain 1 kg of fat after 77 days. 7700 calories. And that's if you have the worst genetics imaginable and you store 100% of your excess calories as fat . Now we come to people who have top genetics. I have always assumed that 40% of excess calories are stored as fat. It is now known that up to 25% of excess calories are stored as fat . This means that if I have, for example, the best possible genetics, then I need 308 days to gain 1 kg of fat. But now it gets even more messed up, because our feeling of satiety is also controlled by our genes, and this varies in intensity. Now, if you imagine what the ideal feeling of satiety actually is? That's when we know exactly, "Right now I've eaten as much energy as I really need, and then I'm full and I don't need to eat anymore." The problem is also that this is expressed differently in the population due to different gene variants, and it means that there are people who do not really have this feedback mechanism . They get full much later than they should. This means they simply eat much more and their intuition is lying to them. Now it gets even more messed up, namely our reward system. One reason is that the receptor density of DRD2, a doping receptor, is lower, resulting in a lower reward from eating and therefore intuitively eating more than one should in order to get the same reward as someone else who eats less of the same thing. But now there's also the opposite perspective, showing how this can lead to obesity, namely that eating itself is extremely rewarding and thus a kind of addiction reinforcement takes place. This means there is a very strong release of doping. This simply happens when you lose weight very quickly, and to be fair, it's not something that only happens to Osempsiert, but also, for example, if you have a competitive bodybuilder who dehydrates and loses weight very quickly and drastically, and their face simply collapses because they lack fat deposits . On the one hand, it is known, for example, that gallstones can also develop in cases of crashes . Osemp too. Most of the time, such gallstones are harmless, unless there are so many of them or they clog the ducts, which can then lead to pancreatitis. Otherwise, eye problems can also occur in diabetics due to fluctuations in blood sugar levels. One of the side effects that occurs in all cases is a loss of between 25 and 33% of muscle mass. This means that if you weigh 100 kg and lose 15 kg in a year , you will lose up to 5 kg of muscle. Well, if you watch the channel, then you know how important muscles are, both as a metabolic organ and to perform all your daily functions. Muscles are extremely important and one of the predictors for a healthy life, but also for staying alive. The big problem is that if you take Osemp and then stop, the most messed-up yo-yo effect in the world begins. Now you're saying that you only regain two-thirds of your body weight. That means the weight I've lost is muscle. The fat comes back, and that's really not something I would recommend to anyone . This is extremely problematic. Secondly, rapid weight loss results in a progressively lower mechanical load on our skeletal system, leading to increasingly lower stimuli, which the bones then adapt to. This means that if you are a little heavier, and your legs have become super strong because you have to climb stairs, for example, your bone density will also be better because it has become accustomed to carrying around a heavy body. If it suddenly drops drastically, the bone simply adapts to these conditions. Plus, it also lacks the nutrients to build up, resulting in a very accelerated loss of bone density, which leads to a side effect in many people that they hadn't expected at all, and which is why they often stop taking it . The thing about bones and muscles is clear, but what also happens in everyday life, and what is really noticeable, is that yes, you do lose weight and you are less hungry, but that also means that you have less energy available, and here there are two problems that you have to pay particular attention to. And if the motivation to eat food decreases, and we eat less, then we also massively increase the risk of developing nutritional deficiencies. However, the side effects of being overweight or having type 2 diabetes are significantly worse than the side effects of the medication . The doctor's risk assessment is then such that it makes sense to give you this medication, and the doctor can be quite right if you are prescribed it for these reasons. - Slow Weight Loss on GLP-1s? How to Break Your Plateau: Kind: captions Language: en If you're taking GLP-1 medications like Wiggoi, Zeppbound, or Ozmpic for weight management, you might be thinking about why some people seem to lose weight faster or more significantly than others. Perhaps you've seen incredible transformations online, but your own path feels a bit different. It's natural to ask, what factors are truly at play here, and what can you actually control to optimize your results? So today, we're going to break down the science behind the varying responses to GLP-1 medications, looking at both the things you can influence and those you can't, so you can approach your weight loss path with clarity and confidence. Just a quick reminder, this video is forformational purposes only and isn't medical advice. Always talk with your prescribing doctor or pharmacist about your specific health needs and treatment plan. The truth is, weight loss is a complex process and GLP ones are powerful tools that work differently for everyone, but there's a lot you can do to support their effectiveness. All right, let's start by looking at some of the factors that are largely outside of your direct control. First up are the uncontrollable factors that shape your individual response to GLP1 medications. Your genetics play a significant role. Our genes influence everything from our metabolism and how our bodies store fat to our natural hunger and satiety signals and even how we respond to specific medications. Some people are simply predisposed to lose weight more readily than others regardless of the medication. Then there's your baseline health. Conditions like PCOS, thyroid disorders, or significant insulin resistance can impact weight loss even with GLP ones. The presence of other medications you might be taking, such as certain anti-depressants, corticosteroids, or even some diabetes medications, can also affect your weight and potentially counteract some of the GLP ones effects. Things like age and sex can also play a part as metabolic rates and hormonal profiles naturally differ. While these factors are important, remember they don't mean you can't achieve meaningful results. They just mean your path might look a little different from someone else's. Now, let's shift our focus to what you can actively influence. Starting with your food choices and protein intake. While GLP1 medications work to reduce your appetite and slow digestion, they aren't a license to eat whatever you want. What you put into your body still matters immensely. Prioritizing nutrient-dense foods is key. Think lean proteins, fiber richch vegetables, fruits, and whole grains. These foods provide essential vitamins and minerals, keep you feeling full, and support overall health. Among these, protein is especially critical. When you're losing weight, particularly rapid weight, there's a risk of losing muscle mass along with fat. Adequate protein intake, helps preserve that precious muscle, which is vital for maintaining a healthy metabolism. Aim for a good source of protein with every meal, whether it's lean meats, fish, eggs, dairy, or plant-based options like beans, and lentils. Many experts suggest aiming for around 0.8 to one gram of protein per pound of your ideal body weight each day. Avoiding ultrarocessed foods, sugary drinks, and excessive amounts of unhealthy fats will also make a significant difference in optimizing your GLP1 results. Remember, the goal is still to create a calorie deficit, but doing it with nutritious foods will yield better, more sustainable outcomes. Next, let's talk about the powerful trio of movement, sleep, and hydration. These three lifestyle factors are often underestimated but are absolutely crucial for maximizing your GLP1 weight loss path. Regular physical activity is vital. It's not just about burning calories. Movement helps build and maintain muscle mass, improves insulin sensitivity, and improves your mood. Try to incorporate a mix of activities. Resistance training like lifting weights or body weight exercises is especially important for preserving muscle as you lose weight. Aerobic exercise like brisk walking or cycling supports cardiovascular health. Don't forget about sleep. Poor sleep can wreak havoc on your hormones, increasing hunger-promoting hormones like ghrein and decreasing satiety hormones like leptin. It also impacts cortisol levels which can affect fat storage. Aim for 7 to n hours of quality sleep each night. And then there's hydration. Staying well hydrated is fundamental for all bodily functions including metabolism. Sometimes thirst can even be mistaken for hunger. So drinking enough water can help manage your appetite and prevent unnecessary snacking. On the flip side, consider your alcohol intake. Alcohol adds empty calories, can impair sleep, and might even increase cravings, potentially working against your weight loss efforts. Making conscious choices in these areas can significantly enhance the effectiveness of your GLP-1 medication. Another vital aspect of optimizing your GLP1 path is medication adherence and setting realistic expectations. Adherence means taking your medication exactly as prescribed by your doctor. This includes taking it on the same day each week if it's a weekly injection or consistently every day if it's a daily dose. Following the prescribed titration schedule, that gradual increase in dosage over time is also incredibly important. This allows your body to adjust to the medication and helps minimize potential side effects. Skipping doses or not following the schedule can reduce the medication's effectiveness and make it harder to achieve consistent results. Just as important are realistic expectations. While GLP1s are highly effective, they are not a magic bullet and individual results vary. Clinical trials show significant weight loss, but not everyone will achieve the maximum reported percentages. Focus on your personal progress and the health benefits you're experiencing rather than comparing yourself to the most dramatic outcomes you might see online. Remember, the goal, as we often discuss on pharmacist online, isn't just weight loss, but sustainable health improvements. It's also important to understand that weight loss progress is rarely linear and plateaus are a completely normal part of the path. Many people expect a steady, continuous drop on the scale, but the body is complex and adapts over time. You might see rapid weight loss initially, followed by periods where the scale doesn't budge for weeks, even if you're doing everything right. This is often called a plateau. And it doesn't mean your medication has stopped working or that you failed. Plateaus can happen for several reasons. As you lose weight, your body requires fewer calories to maintain its new, smaller size, meaning your metabolism might slow down slightly. You could also be experiencing changes in body composition, losing fat while gaining muscle, which can make the scale seem stagnant. Fluid retention, hormonal fluctuations, or even changes in bowel habits can also temporarily mask fat loss. When you hit a plateau, it's a good time to reassess your habits. Are you still consistently making nutritious food choices? Is your movement routine consistent and challenging enough? Are you getting adequate sleep? Sometimes small adjustments can help restart progress. Finally, let's talk about when and how to talk to your healthare team if your results seem off. Quote, "If you feel like you're not seeing the expected progress after a reasonable amount of time, or if you've been stuck in a plateau for an extended period despite consistent effort, it's definitely time to have a conversation. Your doctor or pharmacist can help you evaluate several things. They can check for any underlying medical conditions that might be hindering your progress or review other medications you're taking for potential interactions. They might also discuss your current GLP-1 dosage and whether an adjustment is appropriate or if a different GLP-1 medication might be a better fit for you. Please don't hesitate to share any concerns about side effects as they can impact adherence and your overall well-being. Preparing a list of questions beforehand can make these conversations more productive. Your healthare team is your best resource for personalized advice and adjustments to your treatment plan. They have a comprehensive understanding of your health history and can offer guidance tailored specifically to you. Understanding the factors that influence GLP-1 weight loss, both those you can control and those you can't, empowers you to take a more active and informed role in your health path. By focusing on nutrient-dense foods, prioritizing protein, staying active, getting enough sleep, and maintaining good hydration, you can significantly enhance the effectiveness of your medication. Remember to adhere to your prescribed dosing schedule and manage your expectations realistically, knowing that plateaus are a normal part of the process. If you find this type of detailed, evidence-based health information helpful, make sure you subscribe to Pharmacist Online for more insights into medications and your

- Are We Using GLP-1 Medications All Wrong? with Dr. Ben Bikman: Kind: captions Language: en-US So to me, the best use of this self-discipline in a syringe is to look at it as a temporary enhancement to overcome addictions, rather than this is your new normal and you're going to be drugged for life. What if we're using GLP-1 medications all wrong? Look, the GLP-1 medications, Wegovy, Mounjaro, Ozempic, they are revolutionizing the medical profession of weight loss, right? Like the medical weight loss is beyond anything we've ever seen. But what if we're using them incorrectly? What if to get optimal metabolic health and long-term safety and long-term weight loss, there was a better way to use them? I'm joined today by Dr. Ben Bikman, who's a professor and researcher at BYU. And he specializes in insulin and metabolic health. And he has a really interesting theory about using these medications as microdoses, to help curb cravings for carbohydrates, which will then allow a transition to a healthy low-carb diet, to allow for longer term success with weight loss, but more importantly, improved metabolic health, with very low risk for any side effects and lower cost. Sounds intriguing? Let's get into the details with Professor Ben Bikman. Well, Ben, welcome back to Metabolic Mind. It's great to see you again. Yeah. Hey, Bret, glad to connect with you. Yeah. I enjoy every opportunity to sit down and chat with you. We had a great interview a while back here on Metabolic Mind, which I highly recommend people check out. But I reached out to you to circle back and talk to you again because of this really interesting article you wrote, this op-ed about the use of GLP-1s, sort of microdosing to address cravings. So I want to get into all that. But before we get into the details, tell us a little bit about what got you interested in metabolic health to begin with and maybe how that led you to think about GLP-1s and so forth? Yeah. My journey towards the, well, to the point I'm at now, all of which, of course, always a means to an end as a young married guy thinking about how to provide for a future family, during my master's degree in exercise science or exercise physiology, which really had me thinking that I would have a career devoted to muscle tissue and muscle metabolism. But in the course of that thesis work, I stumbled on a paper, that had just recently been published, this is 25 years ago-ish at this point. And the paper documented a phenomenon that was an absolute revelation to me, which is that the fat cells, can aggressively secrete pro-inflammatory hormones, which in and of itself was fascinating. The fact that the fat cell was an active endocrine organ, I had no idea of that at the time. I'd never learned that. But during the course then of my dissertation, which was my dissertation work with a man named Linus Dome, my work was looking at the degree to which inflammation contributes to insulin resistance. And in fact, Linus, at the time, my mentor, whom I love and revere to this day, he had one of the first grants funded by Johnson and Johnson, to look at the effects of these incretins. And all of the conversation around these gut derived hormones with GLP-1 then and even now still being the most famous, was focused on its role as an anti-diabetic. But there was this side effect, there was this known phenomenon that people on these drugs tended to just eat a little less and would thus lose a little weight. And that became, of course, the fervor that we find the world enraptured with now, which is this idea that the drugs are weight loss drugs. So I've had my finger on the pulse of these drugs for over 20 years now. But I never could have predicted. In fact, indeed, Bret, if I had, I would have made some more prudent investments to secure my family's financial future. I never could have imagined the obsession that has come to surround what had long been known as just a pretty effective anti diabetic. Yeah, I think it's so interesting to go back in your history and the concept of fat being an endocrine organ and actually being able to secrete pro-inflammatory marker or pro-inflammatory hormones and chemicals. And you're right, that's not how we're taught in medical school or undergrad or, you know, fat is just something that is stored calories, basically excess energy. You don't is not thought of so much as being active. And really interesting concept about, you know, GLP-1 have been on the radar screen for a long time, haven't they? We think they're like a brand new drug, but it's a brand new formulation and a brand new use. And there was actually a podcast I heard not too long ago about the GLP-1s about sort of the history of their development and how it was almost scrapped. Like they couldn't quite get it for a long enough half life and a high enough dose. And it sounded like it was so close to being scrapped. But then of course turns into the blockbuster medication that it is and a very effective one. Like we have to be honest, like people are losing more weight on these medications than they've lost on any medication in the past. Now, the question, I would even argue that's all absolutely amen. They're very effective. In fact, that's really why I think we need a little bit of a nuanced view. They're too effective. Right. So, let's talk about that. The fact that you can say people are losing more weight on these drugs than any other drug in the history, most people would take that as a positive thing. But as I say, you don't necessarily see that as an overwhelming positive statement. So help us clarify that. Yeah. So my a lot of my view on the current use of these drugs is that they are they are a little too much of a good thing. So with the use of the drug, we need to appreciate what GLP-1 does. And GLP-1 has myriad effects throughout the body. Yes, on the alpha cells of the pancreas regulating glucagon. Yes, receptors on the intestine regulating the movement of the smooth muscle or in other words, the rate at which food is moving through the gut. But also too, there are effects at the at the hypothalamus in the brain which influence satiety and hunger, in fact, reducing hunger promoting a sense of satiety. And when you combine that central brain effect, of promoting a sense of fullness, with the slower movement of food through the intestines, that is a dynamite combination of helping someone just want to eat less. Now, it is a little too much of a good thing, where you are starting to get there are enough consequences that have been revealed in the published literature that I've, tried to be a voice of caution, but I somewhat regret that some people have have assumed that I am just universally opposed. I'm actually not universally opposed. I just think that I am opposed to how they're currently used, which is that the dose is too high, and that they are used, they are prescribed with the language being this is a weight loss drug. When when I actually think we don't do the patient any good, when we describe it as a weight loss drug, because we ought to be helping using it in a way to improve habits. Now, before I even mention the habit part of it, and in my ideal use of the drug, if used at all, I just want to mention that there are consequences to the currently prescribed use of these drugs, including a significantly elevated risk of blindness that just came out within the last month, a twofold increased risk of blindness, and the consequences on on overall body mass, where a two year trial found that of the total weight lost, roughly 40% was coming from fat free mass. Now that doesn't mean it's all muscle and bone, but it certainly means some of it is muscle and bone. That is sobering in light of the fact that over 70% of patients in the United States, get off the drug at two years, for whatever reason, cost or access, or they get tired of feeling a little nauseous, which is how a person feels when the stomach slows down as much as it does. And what do they gain back? Well, the human body will readily gain back fat. But depending on the age and even the sex of the individual, they may never gain back that muscle and bone mass even as modest as it was, that's gone probably for good. And one last nail in the coffin, I would say is with with regards to why I'm opposed to these drugs, as they're currently used, it's the effect on emotional well being where we often hype up the effects of the food noise where the person's cravings are gone for for junk food for various foods. And we'll revisit that in a moment. But what if the reality is that their cravings for many things they used to enjoy are gone? And what I mean by that is there is an over about a 200% increased risk of major depression in these individuals on the drug versus placebo, as well as an over 100% increased risk of anxiety and even suicidal behaviors. So one way of perhaps more honestly describing the reduced cravings for food they shouldn't be eating, maybe to say that you just aren't going to like anything as much as you used to like. In other words, you have a felt you have a dude who is less interested in getting together with his guys, his friends to go bowling. Or you have a gal who's less interested in going for a walk around the block with her with her girlfriends from her neighborhood or something. And so you have people who just may be less interested in all of the things that used to bring them joy. And this may be the reality that explains the significant effect on mood disorders. So that's why those are some of my main concerns that really, I think justify some heavy caution with the current use of the drugs. Yeah, it's a very interesting point about just less interest in everything and not just food. I hadn't seen so much about that. So it's very interesting to hear about. And we've done content here about the psychiatric potential impacts with with some studies showing no worsening. But all those studies exclude anybody with any kind of a psychiatric history. And those that don't sort of the real world evidence suggests something completely different that there could be an increased risk for those who are predisposed to depression or suicidality or whatever the case may be. So that's really interesting. But just to go back to the muscle loss or the lean body mass loss, it sort of highlights that weight loss isn't necessarily the goal, but improved metabolic health is the goal. And losing fat and gaining muscle is a perfect combination for improving metabolic health. But losing fat and muscle can still improve metabolic health as these studies have shown, but makes me wonder about the long term consequences of that with with decreased muscle mass. And then the throw on top of that, though, some people are saying, well, it's the same thing with weight loss surgery. It's the same thing with severe caloric restriction, you see the same percentage of lean mass loss. So that makes it okay. So I want to get your impression on those two things, both, you know, justifying it that way, and also the impact on long term metabolic health. Yeah, so I do think it is appropriate to compare the use of the GLP-1 drugs to other very often relied on weight loss interventions like gastric bypass and the other one you mentioned. Severe caloric restriction. Very, very low calorie diet. Yeah, yeah. That actually the reason I mentioned that is just from the scientific perspective, which is that it helps us understand how a person is losing this weight, even this good weight, if you will, the lean mass potentially on the drug, because it does suggest that it's not a direct effect of the drug itself, but rather an artifact of the fact that they're just not really eating as much as they were before. And even maybe what they are eating is being very poorly absorbed. That's certainly part of the consequence of so significantly slowing the rate at which food is moving through the intestine. And so just that's an interesting just point for people to take away from how you phrased the question, which is that it does at least bring some comfort to the fact that it's not a direct effect of the drug, for example, or in other words, it doesn't mean the semiglutide is somehow directly damaging muscle tissue. But again, it's rather just a feature of the overall metabolic milieu as someone of someone who is perhaps poorly nourishing their body. Yeah, but further, it's not like, it's not like those interventions severely restricting calories and undergoing gastric bypass don't have a rebound themselves. That we know that the people who attempt to lose weight just through calorie restriction alone, with that being the intention, that's also the intention. That is the mechanism whereby people lose weight on the biggest loser game show. And there's a reason you never see these people again, because once they're off the air, they can't continue this starvation-induced model, and then hunger always wins. Moreover, even the rate at which people regain weight with gastric bypass is quite sobering, where they can start to out eat the physical restrictions of the gut. What was once a very small little stomach because of the surgical restriction of it, the intestines have this remarkable ability to be somewhat dynamic or plastic, if you will, and then they can start to stretch out again with repeated stretching and before the person knows they're back to eating how they were before. With the use of the drug, it's actually even simpler than that, which is that this phenomenon of diminishing returns, where you have too much of a signal. In fact, there is evidence to suggest this, although it's in animals, that with repeated GLP-1 activation, the signal starts to decay. In other words, too much of a signal will result in a resistance to the signal, which is a fundamental principle of biology, whether it's cancer biology and chemotherapeutics, whether it's antibiotics to treat infections, and even hormones, too much of a signal will result in the decay or the loss of that signal over time. And GLP-1 would be no exception to this. So we see the diminishing returns of the drug, which is one of the reasons why I would imagine people are getting off it at such a high rate, because it just stops working like it used to. Right, if you're still getting the side effects and the bill to have to pay for it, but not seeing the benefits, why would you continue it? Yeah. Well, so obviously here at Metabolic Mind, we focus on metabolic health and mental health. And the reason why I like to talk so much about these medications is because they do have an impact on metabolic health, but also that, as you mentioned, they have direct brain effects. And part of that could be for cravings. Like we know a big part of overeating leading to obesity and type 2 diabetes and poor metabolic health is cravings, is just you could say the brain going haywire, so to speak, and it's not necessarily willpower. It's not necessarily just white knuckle it, but your brain is almost being hijacked. So a lot of what you wrote in this article of maybe a new way to use these GLP-1s focused on cravings. So tell us about that, how you got into that. Yeah, yeah, it was a paper so I need to give some credit to Arne Astrup, who is in Denmark. And he has been really at the forefront of GLP-1 research, maybe since its inception. I was at an event with him and he had mentioned a compelling perspective, which now in hindsight seems obvious, but it's not one that I'd considered, which is that he had said, "What if some people just don't have a sufficient GLP-1 response to food and that's what's driving the obesity?" And it was an offhand comment, and I'm sure for him it wasn't, and he was thinking of a dozen studies that he could readily cite, although none of which would have been familiar to me because it was such a novel view for me. But to say all this another way, there was a paper published in the Journal Gut by by Ranganath et al and anyone could look this up. It is fascinating! What they did in 1996, which Bret was a wonderful year to graduate from high school, class of 96, but also a fascinating year to make a seminal discovery in the relevance potentially of GLP-1 when it comes to obesity and its origins. Neither you nor I are saying that calories don't matter, but you and I both have a more nuanced view, not to put words in your mouth, but that there needs to be an endocrine component that allows the fat cell or that enables the fat cell to even store that energy in the first place. And that would be the hormone insulin. And so when you appreciate the necessity of insulin telling the fat cell to store that energy, and then the need for sufficient calories to fuel that storage into the fat cell, it does leave you with a unique perspective that has you say, "Okay, well then what will spike insulin the most?" And of course that's going to be dietary carbohydrates. Now to bring all of this back to GLP-1, and this Ranganath study in the journal Gut in 1996, they took people and separated them into two groups. So we imagine two people, now I'm oversimplifying, but one is lean, one is obese. They eat a carbohydrate heavy meal. The lean guy eats that same amount of carbohydrate, and his brain has told him, "You're done. You don't need more." And so he pushes the plate away. He's done. He gets up and he leaves. But his buddy, his roommate, his college roommate eats that same amount of carbohydrate, and he does not get the signal telling him to stop. In fact, not only does he not get the stop signal, as GLP-1 goes down, which it did at around two to three hours, not only did he not stop, he may want more. And now we have this, I think an alternate, a different paradigm here when we look at GLP-1 and its potential utility. Because what if based on that study and others like it, rather than saying, "We're going to give you this drug for weight loss," the individual thinking, it is just a magic intervention that they can still eat whatever they want, but the drug is going to help them lose weight, which is not true. The drug helps the person lose weight as it changes habits. And what is the habit that it perhaps changes the most when it comes to diet? It is controlling carbohydrate consumption or the cravings. Because within the scope of all of the neurobiology research of cravings, there is no evidence that humans crave fat. There is no evidence that they crave protein. There is significant and consistent evidence that they crave carbohydrates. Now, someone would say, "Well, with fat makes it even better." Yeah, but still, carbohydrates are the thing that they crave the most. And we intuitively know this. No one is sitting down and craving a hard-boiled egg, a perfect balance of fat and protein. We crave carbohydrates. The saltier and crunchier, the better. Or the sweeter and the gooier, the best of all, perhaps. But it is going to be a carbohydrate. And so what if our use of the drug influences the conversation that the clinician has with the patient? And that is maybe to sum it all up in a crystallized way here. It would be that I would imagine the best use of the drug being a clinician who sits down with the patient and then explain, "The patient doesn't need to be told they're overweight or unhealthy because of it." They would know intuitively. And it is important that the clinician acknowledge it, and not pretend that having too much body fat is benign. But they say, "You need to change the way you're eating." And this even comes back to an earlier part of our conversation, Bret, where I said, "As much as you and I can both acknowledge the calories matter, that should not be the beginning of the conversation. I believe because that leads to hunger and just pure restriction. What we ought to say is, "Let's put you on a program that's going to help lower your insulin." Because when you lower insulin, the body will be burning more fat, and metabolic rate will go up potentially Thus, we say, "Don't worry about your calories, just control your insulin. And to do that most effectively, control your carbohydrates, which is, stop getting your carbs from bags and boxes with barcodes." Whole fruits and vegetables, enjoy them liberally, eat them, don't drink them, but stop getting these refined ultra-processed carbs. And then the seed oil crew should nod their heads to that because that's how we mostly get our seed oils at the same time within those packages of refined carbs. Right. So that puts an extra layer on sort of what you were saying about this study, though, because, you know, same time we're saying that the GLP-1 did not go up with the carbs and they were craving carbs. At the same time, they're not craving probably broccoli and beans or lentils, right? They're craving the more refined carbs and the processing, which is actually really interesting because now, you know, so many people are focusing on the processing itself and it's the processing and maybe focusing less on the fact that it's carbohydrate and its effect on insulin. So there does seem to be like a little bit of a balance, but it seems like GLP-1 might be the equalizer for that. Do you think it because it addresses both? Yeah, yeah, that's right. I think so. Bret. In fact, that question sort of allows me to sort of complete that, this whole long ranting thought, which is that, I think the best use of the drug is when the clinician tells the patient, "You need to control your carbs." But there will be some people who will say back to the clinician, maybe even after having attempted this for some time, they will say, "I just can't do it. I am trying to control my carbs." Then the clinician can with nothing but empathy say, "All right, to some degree or another, you're addicted. Now let's help you with your addiction." And so here is a drug, at what we could call a microdose, a fraction of what it's commonly used at now. Then I'm going to cycle you on this for, I don't know, 90 days. Then during this time, we'll be checking in with you to determine how well you're doing at controlling your carbohydrate cravings, the thing that humans crave the most when it comes to nutrition. Many people, I have known, this is anecdotal, which pains a scientist to invoke, but I've known individuals who do a 90-day protocol and when they wean themselves off, they cycle off the drug, the habits have persisted. And there is something powerful about that 90 days when it comes to changing habits and breaking addictions. Some people will find that they can get off and they have a new normal. They've rewired their habits, if you will. Some people will be good, but then they'll find the cravings starting to come back. Okay, no problem, cycle them back on. And so overall, my view and the perspective that I attempted to articulate in that article, which was a good experience for me, a good exercise to actually crystallize these thoughts, it is that the drugs are very powerful and thus could be effective at a lower dose if framed in the context of "This is a drug that's helping you control your cravings." not framed as a weight loss drug. And then second, presenting the idea that this is a temporary intervention. You're not on this forever. So you need to be deliberate about the habits that you're changing in the decisions you're making, because in 90 days, I'm going to want to start to cycle you off this, to see how well the habits have become a new normal for you. self-discipline in a syringe is to look at it as a temporary enhancement to overcome addictions, Right. And not just, "Well, here now you're going to eat less of the standard American diet or now you're going to eat less of the very high carb diet, but rather this is the way to curb your cravings and your addiction so that you can transition to a lower carb diet, which will then impact your insulin and your fat burning and lead you to better metabolic health. And as you pointed out, and so have I, in numerous studies, the weight loss from a ketogenic diet is generally preserving of the lean mass or certainly losing a lot less of the lean mass. So it seems like this is a way to really move towards greater metabolic health. Is that how you see it as well? Yeah, yeah, yeah, right. In fact, that's a great ending there, as you said it, because earlier you'd sort of phrase this question of, "Are all weight loss interventions going to result in the same degree of lean mass loss?" And no, if you can be adequately nourishing the body with all of what it needs, vitamins, minerals, et cetera, and not driving it into an overt caloric deficit, but yet insulin is low, you are creating an environment that does facilitate uniquely fat loss versus muscle loss. And so the, in fact, my lab published a report finding that ketones are actually muscle preserving added as a direct signaling molecule that where beta-hydroxybutyrate actually inhibited the, we gave it the muscles, a chemical insult and beta-hydroxybutyrate actually made the muscle cells more robust and resistant to injury. So we have evidence right at the level of the cell, not to mention the significant commentary of decades of work from Dr. George Cahill, who would often refer to ketones as the great muscle preserving molecule that they're muscle sparing. But yeah, I think it's not a stretch to then say with a ketogenic low carb diet, with the intention being I'm just lowering my insulin, I'm not lowering my calories per se, you're going to find I think yourself in a very superior metabolic environment to promote selectively greater fat loss. Yeah. And I think that's so important to emphasize that it's not weight loss, it's fat loss, especially visceral fat, maintaining lean muscle mass, and you just set it up perfectly for a way to do that. Now, you know, a lot of Fellow mental health clinicians and health care providers, you now have access to a suite of free CME lectures on metabolic psychiatry and metabolic health. Now back to the video! studies that involve pharmaceuticals and medications are sponsored and paid for by the pharmaceutical company. And that's why they get done quickly and with, you know, thousands and thousands of people. But for what you're proposing, it probably wouldn't be to the benefit of the pharmaceutical company, because it's a much lower dose. And for a short time, as opposed to the current high dose, take it forever, see you later, which obviously benefits the pharmaceutical company. So do you think labs will start doing research studies on this, they'd have to get NIH funding or philanthropy funding or whatnot, but do you think more will be doing it? And will your lab? Yeah, yeah, yeah, good question. So we are currently actually analyzing a data set from a clinic. So I had a clinician reach out to me, who has been using low dose semiglutide with a combined low carb diet and their results outperform even the reported results of just semiglutide alone. And again, what the patient finds so enjoyable is the fact that they're not expected to be on the drug indefinitely. I think that is I think that's common as much as we have a culture, particularly in the US, where people are able to see advertisements for drugs so much more readily than almost any other in any other country on the planet. We certainly have a more drug friendly culture in clinical care, where the patient comes in asking for a drug, which is very odd in the rest of the world, because they've seen an ad. But even then, I don't think a person would want to be on the drug indefinitely. So this all comes back to what I mentioned earlier, that I think a lot of the value in this alternative paradigm of the use of the drug, namely micro dosing and cycling, is that it changes the purpose of the drug that the person is looking at it as, you are a temporary tool, you little syringe, to help me learn to change my habits. But that still requires some effort on their part. It's not it's not depriving them of the benefit of learning that self discipline, which I think is one of the points of life in a kind of grander sense, which is to just there's, you know, just to me telling my body, "Hey, you fleshy tabernacle of clay, you're not in charge. I am. The part of me that is beyond this fleshy body." And I don't like and I think it's human nature. We don't like feeling addicted to things. We don't like feeling dependent on things. Even if it is the intervention that's helping us curb the addiction, we would like to get over both of them. We want to stop using the intervention because we've learned to control our addictions. Yeah, yeah. I think that's really well said. Yeah. And I think the way you're proposing it is almost like a, I don't know, like a no brainer. Like why not Why wouldn't you try this first, right? Rather than giving somebody a large dose of a medication that has serious potential side effects and can likely sort of commit them to lifelong use. a lower dose safer, shorter term to see if it helps kickstart their own sort of lifestyle changes? And I'm going to answer my own question here because I think part of that is, doctors have sort of become jaded. They're like, "Ah, diet doesn't work. Lifestyle interventions don't work because what they've been taught and tried for so long, they eat less, move more, low fat. Yeah, it hasn't worked for 50 years." So they get sort of jaded. But so that's the other part that's really important to emphasize of what you're saying is it's as a transition to reducing the carbohydrate. So yeah, why wouldn't they do that? I think people should do it more. Well, and I think it's a combination you would know, of course, having gone through medical training. I think it's a combination of the fatigue that a physician may experience, the fact that they can't bill that time, you get paid for what you can bill. And talking about nutrition might not be something a physician is able to get compensated for. So I really appreciate that. But at the same time, they might not have even learned anything other than eat less exercise more. If that simplistic mantra worked, we would have solved the obesity problem before it ever started, because we've been saying that for 60 years. Obviously, a weight loss strategy that is based on just straight calorie deprivation and restriction and the sort of mistaken view of the laws of thermodynamics in a biological system, it does not lead to an actual successful intervention. That's standard protocol. Your dosage should be personalized to your body, not a calendar. At Regenix, we track three things before any increase. Number one, your response and side effects. Number two, your weight loss progress. And number three, your personal input. If you're losing 1 to 2 lb per week and feeling good at a lower dose, there's no reason to push higher. More medication isn't always better. Higher doses mean more side effects, greater tolerance, and a harder time tapering off later. The goal isn't to stay on the highest dose possible. It's to find the lowest effective dose that works for your body. Mistake number two, thinking GLP-1s replace healthy habits. Here's a hard truth. The medication suppresses appetite, but it doesn't fix metabolism. You should eat close to 1 g of protein per pound of your desired body weight. Protein protects muscle, and muscle is your metabolism. GLP-1s slow digestion by design, so supporting your gut health is essential. Here's what works best for our patients. Hydration, half your body weight in ounces of water daily. Mistake number five, not checking your blood work. You can't optimize what you don't measure. Weight loss alone doesn't tell the whole story. So you're basically guaranteed to never have that level of heavy lifting from the medication. So you need to learn how to do some heavy lifting on your own. And if you're in the underdosing, you need to make small patient increases. So hopefully you never even get to overdosing. So here's what it looks like when you are in the sweet spot. All right, so let's go. Okay, so just a quick mention about methodology. Each of these statistics was the total aggregate number of people that complain of a side effect and then I've subtracted the placebo effect. This gives us the real attributable risk from the medication. So the most common side effect by far for Zeppang was nausea and that was noticed [clears throat] in 19.5% of users. That is precisely because of these side effects profiles. It gives your body time to adjust and adapt. But if you still run into trouble on the manufacturer suggested escalation, there's no reason that you can't go even slower. So, for example, I've had some patients where I'll maintain them on a starting dose or middle dose for several months before trying to increase it again. Number two, stick yourself more often. The instructions tell you to take this injection once a week, but in some cases, some of my patients have had benefit by dividing the dose and giving themselves two needles per week, half and half, thereby reducing their overall exposure at the beginning of the week. This strategy works particularly well for people who notice that they feel these symptoms the first few days after each shot. And the third method is to stretch it out. What that means is whatever the last dose you could tolerate is, you stay on that dose and don't bother trying to escalate further. You'll still get some of the benefits in terms of weight loss, it just won't be as spectacular as if you could get up to the full dose, but it's still going to be better than nothing, right? Indication and safety summary with warnings. Mjaro is an injectable medicine for adults with type 2 diabetes used along with diet and exercise to improve blood sugar glucose. It is not known if mjaro is safe and effective for use in children. Warnings. Maljaro may cause tumors in the thyroid, including thyroid cancer. Watch for possible symptoms such as a lump or swelling in the neck, horarsseness, trouble swallowing, or shortness of breath. [music] If you have any of these symptoms, tell your health care provider. Do not use Maljaro if you or any of your family have ever had a type of thyroid cancer called meillary thyroid carcinoma, MTC. Do not use mangjaro if you have multiple endocrineasia syndrome type 2me 2. Do not use mangjaro if you're allergic to it or any of the ingredients in mangjaro. Mjaro may cause serious side effects including inflammation of the pancreas pancreatitis. Stop using mangjaro and call your healthcare provider right away if you have severe pain in your stomach area abdomen that will not go away with or without vomiting. You may feel the pain from your abdomen to your back. Low blood sugar, hypoglycemia. Your risk for getting low blood sugar may be higher if you use Mjaro with another medicine that can cause low blood sugar such as a sulfonyl ura or insulin. Signs and symptoms of low blood sugar may include dizziness or lightadedness, sweating, confusion or drowsiness, headache, blurred vision, slurred speech, shakiness, fast heartbeat, anxiety, irritability or mood changes, hunger, weakness, and feeling jittery. Serious allergic reactions. Stop using Mjaro and get medical help right away if you have any symptoms of a serious allergic reaction including swelling of your face, lips, tongue or throat, problems breathing or swallowing, severe rash or itching, fainting or feeling dizzy and very rapid heartbeat. Dehydration leading to kidney problems, diarrhea, nausea and vomiting may cause a loss of fluids, dehydration which may cause kidney problems. It is important for you to drink fluids to help reduce your chance of dehydration. Severe stomach problems. Stomach problems, sometimes severe, have been reported in people who use Mjaro. Tell your health care provider if you have stomach problems that are severe or will not go away. Changes in vision. Tell changes in vision during treatment with Mjaro. Gallbladder problems. Gallbladder problems have happened in some people who use Mjaro. [music] Tell your health care provider right away if you get symptoms of gallbladder problems which may include pain in your upper stomach, abdomen, fever, yellowing of skin or eyes, jaundice, and clay colored stools. Food or liquid getting into the lungs during surgery or other procedures that use anesthesia or deep sleepiness, deep sedation. Mjaro may increase the chance of food getting into your lungs during surgery or other procedures. Tell all your health care providers that you are taking Mjaro before you are scheduled to have surgery or other procedures. Common side effects. The most common side effects of Mjaro include nausea, diarrhea, decreased appetite, vomiting, constipation, indigestion, and stomach abdominal pain. These are not all the possible side effects of Mjaro. Talk to your health care provider about any side effect that bothers you or doesn't go away. Tell your health care provider if you have any side effects. You can report side effects at 1800 FDA1088 or www.fda.gov/medwatch. Before using Mjaro, your health care provider should show you how to use Mjaro before you use it for the first time. Talk to your health care provider about low blood sugar and how to manage it. If you take birth control pills by mouth, talk to your healthcare provider before you use Mjaro. Birth control pills may not work as well while using Mjaro. Your health care provider may recommend another type of birth control for 4 weeks after you start MJRo and for 4 weeks after each increase in your dose of Mjaro. Do you take any other prescription medicines or over-the-counter drugs, vitamins, or herbal supplements? How to take? Read the instructions for use that come with Mjaro. Do not inject mjaro into a muscle intramuscularly or vein introvenously. Use mjaro one time each week at any time of the day. Do not mix insulin and mjaro together in the same injection. - What to know about GLP-1 drugs and muscle loss: Kind: captions Language: en A recent poll from KFF revealed that one in eight adults in the US are currently taking a GLP-1 weight loss drug. And as more people start using them, some are talking about the unexpected side effects, including muscle loss and weakness. According to Vox, patients using GLP-1 drugs described experiencing extreme weakness while trying to lose weight. So, what do people need to know? Dylan Scott, a senior correspondent with Vox, joins me now. Also, Dylan, you wrote about people on these weight loss drugs, the fatigue, the weakness they weren't expecting that came with it. How common was that? And did you find out why it was happening? >> Yeah. So, this is something that clinicians and doctors have known about for a while, but I don't think it's something that's really penetrated to the broader public, especially as you say, more and more people are starting to take them. So, I spoke with doctors and experts to understand what was going on. But the most interesting interesting thing to me was hearing people's personal stories. And if you search some of the internet forums while GOP1 patients get together, swap tips, um talk about their experiences, it was I found dozens upon dozens of examples of people who said they experienced surprising muscle loss and weakness. And there were two people in particular who stuck out to me who I think kind of capture the spectrum here. There was one person who from what they said clearly, you know, had not been a healthy eater, didn't really have a exercise routine, but there was somebody in their early 30s who said that they had trouble walking up the stairs after being on one of these drugs for a while. And on the other end of the spectrum, there was somebody who described themselves as a CrossFit enthusiast, a healthy eater, who nevertheless was like, "I feel flabby. I feel like I'm losing strength. What is going on?" And so I mean I would like to be clear that I think you know I understand why people are excited about these drugs. They are remarkably effective in helping people lose weight and with some of their other properties like anti-inflammation they could be able to treat a wide variety of medical conditions. But there's no such thing as a med magic pill. There's no, you know, drug that you can take that's going to give you exactly the body that you want without you having to put any effort into it. And I think that that's a message that people need to hear if they're going to make the most out of these exciting new medications. >> Well, way to be a Debbie Downer. No, I'm just kidding. I'm just kidding. Is there a way to stave this off? You I mean, you also talked to clinicians and health experts about the right way to take these drugs. >> Yeah. And trust me, I feel that way half of the time. But I Yes. I talked to some experts, including research who have who have tried through animal models and other exper experimental means to understand why does this happen? And it does seem like a big part of it, which makes total sense when you stop and think about it, is that just that people are eating less. They are therefore they have less fuel, less energy to do things, and therefore they feel weaker. And so some of this is the actual loss of muscles. But some of this may also be that people are just not eating enough to have their normal level of activity. So the best way to try to combat this is a combination of two things, and it's the oldest advice in the book. It's try to eat healthy, high protein, low sugar, low-fat diets. And you got to exercise, you got to move, you got to try especially to do strength training training to make sure that your muscles don't start to uh shrink or become weaker. So for some, you know, that can be 150 minutes a week for moderate exercise, walking, swimming, something like that. Maybe 75 minutes a week uh if it's more rigorous. But good diet, good exercise, make sure that you're um doing some kind of strength training. That should allow people to hopefully guard against, you know, experiencing unexpected weakness and and being freaked out by what's happening to their bodies when they're on these drugs. >> All right, Dylan Scott, thank you.

That has been successful for me to maintain my weight around 153, 155 lbs, which is where I am right now. But that's sort of a protocol that I came up with on my own that is not like based in like research. I did find one study that was done in Denmark where 2,200 people reached their target weight with semaglutide and then they started to slowly taper the medication over a period of weeks and they paired that tapering with very very intense lifestyle coaching work around emotional eating coaching around exercise and people were able to maintain the weight loss at the six month mark even after getting off the medication. But that was like with really intense behavioral change interventions. I mean, that's really the bottom line. Is your nutrition dialed in? If you've lost a significant amount of weight, your metabolism is simply slower than if you had never gained the weight in the first place. So, if you take two people, they weigh 130 lbs. The person who never lost weight or never was overweight has a faster metabolism than the person who was overweight and then lost the weight. - GLP-1s Don't Fix Food Noise...They Mute It: Kind: captions Language: en If you are not willing to take GLP1s for the rest of your life, then you need to plan beyond it. GLP1s don't fix food noise. They just turn the volume down. And if you don't learn how to deal with that noise while the volume is quiet or a little more muted, then guess what? The moment the noise comes back just because you're off your prescriptions or because you're no longer taking GLP1s, you know what else comes back? The weight. Hey, I'm Shelly, founder of the Mindful Nutrition School, national board certified health and wellness coach, and proud loser and keeper offer of 60 pounds. and I make educational videos about mindful eating, plant-based recipes, and the benefits of changing your relationship with food. If you're new here, welcome. And if you've been here, welcome back. Let's get into the video. So, if you're taking a GOP1 or if you're considering taking a GLP1 specifically for weight loss, this is something that you really need to understand before you go any further.
