And for the first time, you are feeling that freedom. You feel free. And I get it because I know what it's like to have your mind constantly occupied by food. I'm going to share more about that later. But first, let's talk a bit about food noise. Hunger. Hunger is your body talking, but food noise, that's your mind. Oh, that's your mind. It's that that persistent, sometimes even intrusive preoccupation with food, and it goes beyond normal responses to like seeing a food cue or experiencing hunger. It can exist on a spectrum. So, it could be a very specific craving for fries or an allconuming call for food. It's that voice that's telling you what to eat, when to eat, where to eat, where to buy it. Like, as you're eating your meal, you're thinking about the next meal, you're you're planning, you're you're craving, you possibly are even rerouting your day just for a bite of something delicious. Now, that's food noise. So, if you've ever struggled with emotional eating, stress eating, or even overeating, then you know exactly what I'm talking about. So, I'm not speaking from a place of just science. If I'm speaking from a place of understanding and experience, but speaking of science, let's talk about it for a minute. Let's ground this in what GOP ones actually do. Think back to the last time that you really had a satisfying meal. Like, was it so good, right? You enjoyed every single bite and then the person who served it to you was like, hey, you want some more? And then you're like, oh my goodness, it was so good, but I honestly cannot take another bite. That is what we call fatiety. That is the feeling of being full. So there's these little cells that live in your digestive tract from your stomach to your rectum and they're called entareroindocrine L cells and they're more densely populated around your intestine. And they have one job and their job well they probably have multiple jobs but the job that we care about is that they're going to sense when food comes down. All right? So when they sense food coming down, they're like, "Oh, let's start releasing GLP-1s." Now GLP-1s are the molecules that tell your brain, "Hey, I've had enough. Let's start slowing down digestion." It helps regulate blood sugar and it also helps you feel full. And every time you eat, these hormones rise and then once you stop eating, they go back down. So you go through this process. Your body naturally produces this. It's not something that is out of the ordinary. It's a part of your natural design. And essentially the pharmaceutical companies, they haven't created anything new with GLP1s. They're just amplifying it. And so the difference between your naturally produced GLP1 and the manufactured GLP1s are that the pharmaceutical ones keep that I'm full signal turned on way longer than your body would naturally do. So, your body will feel like it's already eaten even when it hasn't. And that's why your appetite tends to drop when you're on a GLP-1 medication. But here's what we really need to digest about this. That doesn't fix your relationship with food. GOP1s are not fixing things. They're just changing your physical experience of hunger and they're also quieting your mental experience with food noise. But food noise is not a hunger problem. It's a relationship problem. What do I mean by this? So, there was a time in my life where food took up a lot of mental space. Like from the moment I woke up, I'd be thinking about, oh, what am I going to eat today? And then while I was eating my breakfast, I would be thinking about, oh, what am I going to eat for lunch? Maybe I'll pack a snack. What will it be? And so, I'm already thinking about the next meal. And then randomly throughout the day, especially if I had one of those days I was like, "Get me out of here." And I wanted an escape, I'd be looking up recipes, looking up restaurants, figuring out like, "Okay, I'm going to reroute my entire day to go to my favorite bakery or I'll go pick up ingredients from the farmers market so that I can prepare this particular thing." And then I'd be thinking about where I'm going to buy it and what I'm going to buy and then how to prepare it and what I'm eating it. And then I'd be eating it. And the next thing you know as I'm eating it, the cycle would begin again cuz I'd be thinking about the next meal. And for me, the real shift didn't come from changing what I ate nor taking a drug. It came from changing how I related to food. So in 2012, I weighed the most I've ever weighed. And a year later, I dropped 60 lbs. And I've kept it off ever since. Yes. Before that, I would yo-yo up and down trying to find a perfect diet. I took every cleanse, every green juice detox. I would do all the fast, fast for days, not eat any food. I remember one time I drank water for 10 days, I did a liquid fast for 21 days, and I dropped weight. But it was all a quick fix. But here's what significantly shifted my relationship to food. It was when my father died. And I remember that there was this one specific moment that I was wanting to numb the pain with food. And at the time I was living in Atlanta and I was planning to go to Cammy Cakes. I was going to get half a dozen cupcakes and I was going to work out this pain bite by bite. And then I heard this voice say, "Shelly, there is no amount of food that will bring your daddy back again." And that moment changed everything for me. That's when I committed to something different because I accepted it as truth. Cupcakes aren't going to change things, but my relationship to food will. I call it mindful nutrition now, which is kind of similar to mindful eating, but it it goes beyond mindful eating. And I've talked a bit about that on this channel, but back then mindful eating wasn't a trend. So it wasn't something that I could like research and you know find out how to do. It didn't come to me that way. It was just when I accepted it, these three simple practices downloaded into me. I ate when I was hungry. I stopped when I was full. And if I wasn't hungry, but I still wanted to eat. Food noise cravings, then I got curious. What am I actually needing right now? Because that voice, that food noise, it's not random. It was trying to tell me something. I used to call my food noise uh my inner hea. She was so loud and annoying. She'd tell me to go get a brownie, a cupcake, girl, go get a hamburger, and make a blender. Ooh, cheesy pasta. Like, you name it. And she knew. She knew all my restaurants, all the recipes, all the grocery stores, all the brands that I like to eat that would give me that fix. But when I stopped eating every time that she spoke and I began listening to her instead of feeding her, I found out that she didn't want food. She wanted me to deal with something a lot deeper. Emotions, a stressful day, unmet needs, an unhealed past because food was a distraction. And this is why GOP1s are not a complete solution because they do not remove the root. They just mute the signal. So you don't hear the food noise or you don't hear the craving, but that doesn't mean that it's gone. And if you never learn how to understand that signal, how to process it in real time, how to respond to it differently, then when the medication stops, because let's be real, it will, the food noise is going to come back. And you know what else comes back? The weight. So, I'm not here to tell you to not take GOP once. That is absolutely your decision. But what I am saying is this. plan beyond it. What if you learn how to eat for the way that you actually want to live? What if you learn how to eat on vacation as well as at home? What if you learn how to get your needs met without food? What if you learn how to sit with an urge and gift yourself the experience of watching it disappear because it can and it will? I'm a testament to this. What if you learn to listen to your mind and then reason with it without letting it overrun you with thoughts about food? What if you trained your mind to obey your vision instead of randomly serving up impulses? Just what if instead of seeing GLP1s as the solution, you saw it as support, an opportunity to finally learn the patterns without being overwhelmed. And trust me, I get I get that the patterns are overwhelming. But they're not impossible to understand. Because the one thing I want to drive home is that your cravings or your food noise, they're not the enemy. They're messengers. And when you silence him completely, you miss the message. And on top of all that, they even protect your heart. It's no wonder people are calling them a miracle. But, and there's always a butt, isn't there? What if all this amazing progress comes with a hidden price tag? What if, while you're dropping all that weight, these drugs are quietly weakening you? There's this really puzzling side effect that honestly is not getting nearly enough attention. And that brings us to the core paradox of these so-called miracle drugs. Yeah, the weight loss is absolutely real, but the question is, what kind of weight are people losing? because when you dig into that, you uncover a really serious concern, especially for folks who are a bit older or more frail to begin with. So, here's the deal laid out plain and simple. On one side, you've got the incredible promise, the weight loss, the blood sugar control, the heart benefits. Fantastic. But on the other side of the coin, there's this finding that pops up again and again in the clinical trials. People are losing a lot of muscle mass. And that's a big problem because that could mean a faster decline in your ability to just, you know, function day-to-day. And we're not talking about a small amount here. We're talking about your actual muscle, your essential tissues. That is a huge number you just can't sweep under the rug. Now, you might be thinking, "Well, duh. If you eat less, you're going to lose some muscle. And that's true to a point. But the science is showing that's not the whole picture. Not even close. But it turns out its job is way bigger and way more complex than that. A better way to think of it is as your body's chief logistics manager. It's the one coordinating the entire flow of energy and nutrients to make sure the whole system runs like a welloiled machine. Okay, so glucagon has three really critical jobs. Number two, it helps our body handle stress, but number three, and this is the absolute key to our mystery right here, it's in charge of clearing amino acids from our bloodstream. And of course, what are amino acids? They're the literal building blocks for our muscles. Aha. So now the pieces start to click into place, right? By shutting down glucagon to get that great blood sugar control. These drugs accidentally trigger this this hidden nutrient heist. The body is suddenly desperate for those essential amino acids. And with the normal supply chain shut down, it's forced to steal them from the only place left, itself. So let me break down the core mechanism for you. Think of it like a light switch. Normally the glucagon switch is on. Your liver is humming along, pulling amino acids from the food you eat and putting them to good use. Muscles are safe. But with these drugs, that switch gets flipped to off. Suddenly, the liver can't do its job properly. So, the body panics. It needs those amino acids for vital functions. So, it starts raiding your muscle tissue to get them. It's literally breaking down muscle tissue to survive. This completely changes how we have to look at this problem. This isn't just, oh, you lose some muscle when you diet. No, this is a very specific condition caused by the drug. And now researchers are even giving it a new name, a new kind of malnutrition. Okay, the fancy medical term is iatrogenic exposure related malnutrition. I know it's a mouthful. Iatrogenic just means caused by a medical treatment, but what it describes is this dangerous mismatch between the energy and nutrients the body needs versus what it can actually get its hands on, even if you're eating perfectly fine. It's not starvation in the classic sense. It's more like an internal energy crisis all created by how the drug works. And there's this one quote from the research that just nails it perfectly. It says we're getting short-term metabolic gains concealing a progressive erosion of resilience. I mean, think about that. We're looking better on paper. The numbers are great, but underneath the surface, we could be slowly chipping away at our body's ability to bounce back from, well, anything. From stress, from illness, from life. That's where if you regain weight, it comes back as fat, not the muscle you lost. We're talking about becoming more frail, having a harder time healing from an injury, and even having a weaker immune system. It's serious stuff. Okay, I know that sounds pretty serious, and it is. You need structured resistance training, lifting weights, things that tell your body, "Hey, we need to keep this muscle." And finally, you got to monitor things with your doctor, track your strength, maybe even look at your amino acid levels to see what's going on. And what's really exciting is what's on the horizon. A drug called Bogrammob is one of them. The goal is to get rid of this trade-off completely. And that really leaves us with a big final question to chew on. These drugs are an incredible showcase of what modern medicine can do. But they also force us to take a step back and think as we develop these super powerful tools to tweak our own biology. How do we make sure we're striking the right balance? How do we balance getting these amazing immediate results with the much quieter but just as vital goal of protecting our health for the long haul? It's definitely something to think about.

Now, first and foremost, why is maintaining our weight so hard? And to understand that, we need to understand the biology that we're dealing with. You see, your body doesn't want you to stay at your new lower weight. And so, for every kilogram that you lose, it's estimated that your hunger goes up by an additional 100 calories per day. Meanwhile, your metabolism can also slow by about 20 to 30 calories per day. So, not only are you hungrier than ever before, you are also burning fewer calories. And if we then add in today's environment, cheap ultrarocessed foods, sedentary jobs, and Uber Eats being only one little tap away, well, it's going to feel darn near impossible to maintain your weight. Now, before I go any further, I want to make this crystal clear. This is not a willpower problem. It's not that you're lazy or not trying hard enough. For the love of God, if it was, you wouldn't be here watching this video. It's biology plus the environment. You're basically living in a world that is like a 24/7 Costco sample aisle with a brain that keeps screaming, "Feed me." Now, when it comes to the GLP-1 based medications, what we know is that they're extremely effective at helping you to lose weight. But when you come off of that said medication, if we look at a big group of people, what we have found is that 85% of those people ultimately regain the weight and then some when they stop the medication. And again, it's because your biology and the environment are colliding harder than ever before. The medication kept the biology at bay and made the environment much more manageable to navigate. And that is how you ultimately lost weight. You take away the medication and thus you now have the biology and the environment you got to deal with on your own. And this is why we need to treat obesity for what it is, a chronic condition. Just like high blood pressure, just like high cholesterol, GLP-1 medications fix your biology while you're taking them. How's my cravings? How's my hunger? Is my weight creeping up or is it staying nice and stable? If you're doing just fine, well, that might be your new maintenance dose. The fourth point that I look at is don't switch meds if you don't need to. The problem with this whole strategy of things is that it may very well backfire on you. And that's because these drugs are all mimicking the similar pathways and hormones and such like that that are in the body, but there are distinct differences between each molecule. - No One Prepared Me for GLP-1 Maintenance: Kind: captions Language: en-US I was asked something recently that I thought was a really good question. Is maintenance on a GLP-1 hard? My answer to that is, it's not hard, it's just boring. And I don't think that's what people expect. And just so you have some context, I've been on Zepbound for a little over two years now, and I've been in maintenance for almost two of those. So I've had a little, you know, a little time to really sit in this part of it. When people think about maintenance, I think they assume it's going to feel like this completely different phase. Like something is just going to magically shift once you get there. But in my experience, it really doesn't feel that different. When you're losing weight, there's something happening all the time. Even if it's slow, you're still seeing the scale move, you're noticing changes in your body, and your clothes fit differently. There's always something you can point to and say, okay, this is working. And after a while, that just becomes normal. You check the scale, you notice when things change, and you start to expect it. it becomes a part of the process. Even if you're not obsessed with it, it's still there in the background and it's giving you feedback. When you get into maintenance, that feedback just kind of disappears. You're still doing the same things, but nothing is really moving. And I think this is the part that throws people off the most because nothing is telling you that you're doing it right anymore. You're not seeing anything change. So you're left with, Okay, I'm still doing all of this, but how do I know it's still working? I don't think we talk about that part enough. What it feels like to keep doing the same things without anything changing. Because there really is a moment, and if you've been there, you know exactly what I'm talking about. You're just kind of sitting there like, wait, is this it? Because nothing is happening. And that can mess with you a little bit. I honestly think it's because it doesn't feel the way you thought it would. This is where I used to struggle the most. I know that feeling really well because that's exactly how I used to approach it. It was always about getting to a goal as quickly as possible, getting to that magic number, and that was my sole focus. And once I'd get there, I didn't have anything beyond that. I didn't have a plan for what came next. I definitely didn't think about what maintenance would actually look like, so I would go back into my old habits. And of course the weight came back because I treated the whole process like it had an end point, like I could just go back to how things were before. This time for me felt very different because I was intentional. I wasn't looking at this like something that ends. The way that I'm eating now, the way that I'm moving, that's just a part of my normal life. So when I got into maintenance, it didn't feel like I had to switch into something new. It just felt like I had to keep going. And I think that's the difference. If what you're doing feels temporary, maintenance is going to feel hard because you're waiting for it to be over and you're waiting to be done. But if it already feels like you're normal, maintenance doesn't feel like this big shift. It just feels like more of the same. Now, that doesn't mean it's exciting. It's not. There are no big changes or no big wins. It's just you showing up consistently Doing the same thing over and over again And I know that can feel boring But there's no way around it For me, that boring turned into something else It turned into peace For a long time, my habits were very extreme I was either all in or completely off track And there was no in-between So being in the space now where things are just steady, I'm consistent without having to be perfect and it actually feels really good. I honestly didn't expect that. I used to think maintenance would feel like some kind of finish line, but it doesn't. It just feels like normal life. And I think that's what throws people off because it doesn't feel like an achievement anymore. It just feels normal. When people say maintenance is hard, I totally get how I can feel that way. Once you realize that it's just a continuation, you stop looking for something to change and it gets a lot easier because you're not questioning it anymore. You're just doing it. And that's what maintenance is. It's not about chasing anything. So if you're in maintenance right now, I'd really love to hear your experience. Does it feel hard? Or does it just feel normal? - Shedding Weight, Losing Muscle: Study Links GLP-1 Drugs to Reduced Muscle Mass: Kind: captions Language: en Hello and welcome to Medical Dialogues. I am Doctor Bhumika Maikuri. Here is what I bring to you all from the world of medicine. Shedding Weight, Losing Muscle Study Links GLP One Drugs to Reduce Muscle Mass. Popular weight loss medications like GLP One drugs are helping millions lose significant weight. But they may come with an overlook down side. A lack of improvement in heart and lung function. A Key Indicator of Long-Term Health. A new paper published in the Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism by researchers from the University of Virginia (UVA) finds that these medications may fail to enhance cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) despite their other health benefits. GLP-1 drugs widely prescribed to treat obesity, type 2 diabetes, and heart failure are known for improving blood sugar control and reducing short-term heart and kidney- related risks. However, the UVA team found that while patients lose fat, they also experience a substantial loss in fat- free mass, especially muscle, which could impact their cardiovascular health in the long run. The research team reviewed existing studies on GLP-1 drugs and their impact on cardiorespiratory fitness, which is commonly measured through VO2 max. A clinical marker for how well the body uses oxygen during exercise. While GLP one medication improves certain heart function matrix. They did not show consistent and meaningful improvements in V Vito Max. Cardiorespiratory fitness is a potent predictor of all-cause and cardiovascular mortality risk across a range of populations, including obesity, diabetes, and heart failure, said Angadi. A cardiovascular exercise physiologist with the UVS Department of Kinesiology. In a recent study by a group that examined mortality outcomes from almost 400,000 individuals across the world, we found that CRF was superior to overweight and obese status for predicting the risk of death. In fact, once CRF was factored in, body weight failed to predict the risk of mortality. This is why it's so important to understand the ifs of this new class of drugs on it. The authors recommend integrating exercise programs and proper nutrition along with GLP-1 therapy to mitigate muscle loss and improve long- term outcomes. Until more solutions are available. The researchers stress the importance of patient-provider discussions on nutrition, physical activity, and regular screenings for malnutrition and muscle loss risk. That's all for today. - Over 50 on Ozempic? Strength Risks No One Talks About: Kind: captions Language: en If you are over 50 and you have started a GLP1 like Monaro, you might be watching the weight to drop and thinking everything is going well. But at the same time, you may already be losing your strength, your stability, and your long-term health before you even realize it's happening. Because after the age of 50, your body is less able to cope with rapid changes like fast weight loss. And that is where things could start to go wrong. I am Dr. Almary, consultant, weight loss surgeon, and obesity specialist. In this video, I will show you the two hidden risks that could affect your muscle and your bone health, and more importantly, how to protect your body from them so you can stay on the treatment, get the results you want without creating problems that are much harder to reverse later. Let's dive in. Let's start with muscle because this is where things can quietly go wrong. Here is the key fact. When people lose weight on GLP-1 medications, a significant proportion of that weight comes from lean tissue. Not just fat, but muscle. Major trials, including the step one trial and the surount one trial, showed that around 30% of the weight you lose comes from lean mass. That is not just fat. That is muscle. And most people don't expect that. You may have seen this. Someone on GLP-1 looks thinner but less steady, more vulnerable. So in your mind they look better but functionally they are weaker and this is what you need to avoid. So why does this happen? When your biology is in a calorie deficit, it needs energy. So it looks for a fuel and it doesn't just use fat, it uses muscle too. People think if the scale is dropping everything is working right. Well, it's not because after the age of 50, you are already losing muscle every year gradually and GLP1 can accelerate that loss and that is where people get caught out because this kind of loss is much harder to recover from. To put that into perspective, you could lose the equivalent of 20 years of age related muscle loss in just few months. Now you might be thinking why does this actually matter to me? It does because muscle is what keeps you moving. It keeps you steady. It prevents falls. It keeps you independent. And it goes further than that. Lower muscle mass is linked to worse long-term outcomes, including a shorter life expectancy. So, this is not about looking strong. It's about staying capable. Because as you lose weight, you don't just want a lighter body. You want a buddy that still works, that carries you through the years ahead, the ones that you thought you will finally enjoy. Now the weight is coming off. This is about staying independent, avoiding falls, and staying in control of your body as you age. So, how do you stop this happening without giving up the weight loss? Well, you need to follow the muscle preservation system. This is an evidence-based framework built on two foundations. The first foundation is resistant training, weights, resistant bands, body weight exercises, at least two sessions a week. And this is not just about doing the exercise. It's about progressing it. So over time, you need to make it a little bit harder, a little bit more weight, more resistance, or more repetitions. That sends a message to your body. Do not break this muscle down. Preserve it. Strengthen it. I would say this clearly. If you are over 50, do not start GLP1 without a plan for resistance training. This is non-negotiable at home, in the gym, or with a local group, whatever fits your lifestyle. But do not delay it. Plan it, start it. Now, exercise alone is not enough because muscle needs raw material to build and repair and that raw material is protein. And this is the second foundation of your muscle preservation system. Think of protein first when planning your meals. Eggs, fish, chicken, lean meat, Greek yogurt, legumes. But do not miss this point. If protein drops, muscle goes with it. Now that you've got the muscle right, do not overlook the next risk because this one stays hidden until the damage is already done. It is your bones. So, let's go through it next. Now, let's talk about bones because this is a risk that's even easier to miss and often discovered too late. There is emerging evidence suggesting that GLP1 use particularly in older adults may be associated with higher risk of osteoporosis. That means reduced bone density. And this is the condition that increases the risk of fractures particularly hip fractures and spine fractures which are sadly common in older adults and can have a major impact on mobility and quality of life. During rapid weight loss, if bone is not supported, bone strength declines. This is particularly important in women after menopause because they are already at higher risk of osteoporosis and fractures. And this is where it becomes more concerning because osteoporosis is often silent. You don't feel it. You don't see it. There are no clear early warning signs. And for many people, the first time they find out is after a fracture. A simple fall that should have been nothing becomes a fracture that changes everything. And when you look back, the bone thinning was already there, but it was silent. So this is not a condition to react to. This is something you need to anticipate and plan for. So how do you protect your bones while losing weight? You need a clear strategy. Think of this as your bone protection strategy and it has three key pillars. Now the first pillar is loading. We have already covered this. The same resistance training that protects your muscle also protects your bone because your bone responds to load. So when you train, you're not just preserving muscles, you are protecting the entire muscularkeeletal system, including your bones. The second pillar is nutrition. And this is where things quietly go wrong. Your bones need the right nutritions. Calcium, vitamin D, and adequate protein. Now, here is the key point. If you live in the UK or similar regions, for much of the year, you are not getting enough sunlight, which means you're not producing enough vitamin D. And without vitamin D, you cannot absorb calcium properly or use it in the bones to keep them strong. So even if your diet looks reasonable, your bones are not being protected. This is why a simple daily vitamin D supplement is not optional for many people. It's a basic maintenance. Now the third pillar is awareness and monitoring. Remember osteoporosis is a silent condition. So awareness is critical and this is where monitoring matters. If you are in a higher risk group, particularly over the age of 50 or postmenopausal and especially if you're losing a lot of weight on GLP1 without actively protecting your muscles and your bone, then your risk quietly increases. And this is exactly when it becomes important to discuss this with your doctor. You need to consider a baseline assessment with a DEXA scan. A dexa scan is the best investigation to assess your bone density. Once you have this scan, you will know what your baseline is and then you can track it and react early if needed. So if you take one thing from this video, let it be this. GLP1 is powerful, but after the age of 50, your physiological reserve is not the same as it once was. Which means the effects of rapid weight loss, the swings in your system can have much bigger impact on your body. And this is the balance you need to get right because you don't want to win on the scale and losing your body. And that's why you need to get the systems we discussed right. - Insurance and GLP-1s: How to Check Your Coverage | GoodRx: Kind: captions Language: en-US - Compounded medications typically aren’t submitted to insurance, so you’re just paying for them out of pocket. When you’re switching to a branded version, your insurance may not cover it, and the cost can sometimes be higher or there may be a little bit more work and hoops to jump through in order to get them covered. (music) A formulary is a list of the medications that your specific insurance plan covers, and it usually has different tiers, and each tier represents a different cost level or co-pay, or sometimes a higher tier medication may have additional steps that have to be taken before the medication is covered. So knowing what tier it falls in in advance lets you know what you need to prepare for and how to work with your prescriber to get a medication covered so you know what to expect going in. You can access your insurance formulary in a couple of ways. Most are available online, but sometimes that can be a little bit confusing. So I usually suggest calling your insurance company directly and talking to a representative. This is a service you’re paying for, and they have representatives that their job is to work with you to let you know exactly what you need to do to improve your chances of coverage. You can call your insurance company and ask them if it’s covered, what tier it’s in, what the co-pay might be, and if it requires a prior authorization or step therapy or any sort of special steps that you have to take for it to be covered. A prior authorization means that your prescriber has to give some sort of justification to your insurance company for why you need a specific medication, and there’s usually a set number of things the insurance company wants to make sure is the case before they’re gonna approve a medication. Insurance companies may also require something called step therapy, and this means they want you to try a lower-cost medication first before they’ll cover the higher-cost option. Now, both prior authorization and step therapy is something your prescriber is going to work directly with your insurance company to get approved, and this can be confusing because people often call the pharmacy and think the pharmacy knows what’s going on or where the approval process is. The pharmacy only processes it once it’s approved. They don’t really know what’s going on. So you need to either reach out to your prescriber or to the insurance company to know where in the process that approval is. In order to improve your chances of being approved for a GLP-1 medication, you need to have clinical documentation that you have the health condition they’re approved to treat. So that usually requires a prescriber’s visit. It may require some blood tests to show that you actually have these conditions. And sometimes having additional health conditions can improve your case when it comes to the benefit that these medications can provide for you. If your insurance denies coverage for a GLP-1 medication, most insurance companies have an appeal process where you can reach out and get more information as to why it’s not approved and explain why your case may be a little bit different or why clinically you might need this medication to be approved. Now you’re gonna do this in coordination with your prescriber’s office. Your healthcare team is gonna help you with that paperwork and documentation. They usually have a dedicated billing associate that can really work hand in hand with you to get this covered. The second is find out if there’s another GLP-1 that they do cover. Sometimes just switching to a different medication within that class can improve your chances, and then third, if none of those work out for you is to look for patient assistance programs and pharmacy discount cards. They can reduce your out-of-pocket costs to make these medications more affordable. Don’t hesitate to call your insurance company directly. that their job is to work with you to let you know exactly to improve your chances of coverage. They’re not your enemy, so call them and work with them. They really can be helpful resources. (music fades)

- Mounjaro vs Ozempic: Side Effects, Cost, and Safety in Canada: Kind: captions Language: en Mounjaro or Ozempic, which one [music] is right for you? Both are popular injectable medications for diabetes and weight loss, but they're not the same. Here's what you [music] need to know. Ozempic targets one hormone receptor, GLP-1, to reduce appetite, slow digestion, and regulate blood sugar. Mounjaro goes a step further, activating two receptors, >> [music] >> GLP-1 and GIP. The dual action is why Mounjaro tends to show stronger results in clinical trials, especially for weight loss. In head-to-head studies, Mounjaro produced weight loss of roughly 20 to 22% of body fat at highest doses. Ozempic [music] came in at around 15 to 17%. Both are significant, but Mounjaro does have an edge on the numbers. >> That said, individual results vary based on diet, activity, and how well you tolerate the medication. Both medications share a similar side effect profile, mostly nausea, diarrhea, constipation, and vomiting, especially during dose increases. [music] These are usually temporary. Both also carry important warnings around pancreatitis and thyroid health. So, full medical review before starting is essential. [music] Cost-wise, Ozempic is slightly more accessible. Mounjaro tends to run a bit higher per month, and coverage through insurance or drug plans can vary. So, it's worth checking before you make a commitment. Both are evidence-based options, but the right choice depends on your health, >> your goals, and your provider's recommendation.

- Is a GLP-1 Worth It? The Real Cost of Losing 125 Pounds: Kind: captions Language: en Is a GLP1 worth the cost? You might not need a raise to afford your GLP-1. You might just need a reality check. That's what I needed. If you're new here, I'm Megan. I've lost 125 lbs using compounded and a whole lot of hard work. I know what it's like firsthand to look at these medications and think there is no way I can afford that. But when I really sat down and looked at what I was spending before I started this journey, the numbers told a very interesting story. I started in August 2023 weighing 243 lbs. Before I started on this journey, I did not even consider myself obese. I was just moving through my everyday life in the world that I knew. I didn't really think that my weight was a problem until it became a problem. Looking back in hindsight, I can see that I was a fast food addict. At the time, I never realized that and I never would have called myself that. But now, it's so clear to me that there was something so messed up inside of me. When I finally took a look at my habits honestly and started talking to other people in this GLP1 community and realizing that this is not a problem that is unique to only me, but was a problem that I think other people have had. I started to realize that I really was what I would consider an addict. I was eating out five to seven days a week, often multiple times a day. Drive-throughs, takeout, family dinners, ice cream with my kids, you name it. Any excuse I had to eat outside the house, I was doing it. Looking away back in my history, I can see that some of those habits started really early and they just didn't become a problem until later in my life. But over the years, I just started eating out more and more and using that experience of getting fast food as a coping mechanism and a comfort to myself. It didn't happen overnight. It really happened slowly, one tired morning at a time, one busy evening until eating out was just part of my identity. Looking back, I can see that I probably had what would be considered binge eating disorder as well. Well, I was never diagnosed with it, but I was definitely eating more than my body needed to eat. And I was eating not for hunger and for emotional reasons and reasons that were completely out of my control. I used food to cope, to numb, to celebrate, to treat myself. And because I never considered that I had a problem, I didn't know that there were solutions out there for somebody like me. So, the first step wasn't starting a medication for me. It was admitting that I had a problem. Now, maybe you can't relate to my experience of being a fast food addict. Maybe your extra calories look different. Maybe it is a late night snacks after everyone goes to bed, hiding in the pantry, eating a whole box of cookies. Maybe it's sugar and desserts and eating a whole pan of brownies. Maybe it's eating healthy but just eating supersized portions. Whatever form it takes, those of us who are living with obesity are consuming more energy than our bodies need. And here's the truth. None of that is our fault. Obesity is a disease. It's a chronic disease and not a failure of willpower. There are so many mechanisms in the brain and the body that make it incredibly hard to regulate appetite and weight. Conditions like type 2 diabetes, insulin resistance, PCOS can cause our bodies to store fuel as fat even when we're eating normally. And for many of us, there are brainbased factors, dopamine, reward systems, hunger hormones that drive us to eat more than we need. Those causes are real. But those behaviors still have a cost. Every extra bite, every binge, every dessert used to numb for a moment, it all adds up. Not just in calories, but in cold, hard cash. When I finally added it all up and saw how much my habits were costing me financially, physically, emotionally, that's when I realized I could afford a GLP-1. I just had to stop funding the problem long enough to invest in the solution. So, today I want to talk honestly about the cost of being on a GLP-1 medication. Not just the price tag, but how to decide if it's really worth it for you. Because for many of us, it's not about whether we can afford the medication. It's about being honest about whether we can afford not to. Before we dive into the numbers, let's talk about what a GLP-1 actually does. GLP-1 stands for glucagon like peptide. It's a hormone your body naturally produces. It helps regulate blood sugar, lows digestion, and tells your brain that you're full. Some of us unfortunately don't produce enough of this hormone, and that's where these GLP-1 medications can come in. So we have GLP-1 medications like Simaglutide which is the brand name of Ozimpic or WGOI or Tzepide which has a little extra boost to it which has the brand names of Monaro or Zepbound. When you take one of these GLP-1 medications, it mimics that natural hormone in your body. So many of us who are suffering from obesity have things that are out of whack in our body. Our brains are not connected to our stomachs in the way that a normal person experiences the act of eating food. And so when we take this GLP-1, we are more like normal people. Our brain slows down. The food noise quiets. You don't feel that constant pull to food and you finally get space to think before you eat. For me, that changed everything. Having a GLB1 in my brain is what allowed me to slow down the process of my eyes seeing food or a place with food and putting the food in my mouth. Before I had an almost robotic automatic response to food, I had certain triggers. It would be a certain route home where I knew there was a McDonald's and somehow my car would automatically turn into the McDonald's even though I wasn't hungry. I didn't need food. It just it was an automatic response that I had no control over. I could be completely satisfied with the lunch that I ate and when I went to wash my kids dishes, I would finish all the food on their plate, not because I was hungry or the food looked good. It might have had slobber on it and I still ate it. Again, it was a robotic automatic response and my brain did not have enough time to think about that before the food was already in my mouth. I love donuts and I could down many donuts before my brain caught up and said this many donuts is not going to make you feel good later. Now that I have a GLP1 in my brain working on all of these processes, I have enough time to think rationally about food. I can think, am I hungry? Do I like this food and do I want to eat it? Does this food look good to me? Is this food good for me? Is it going to make me feel good and help me reach my goals? All of these questions that I'm asking myself internally that are affecting my behavior around food were completely impossible before I had a GLP-1 on board. Now, a GLP1 didn't give me any new information. I know what it means to eat healthy. I knew all about the food pyramid in second grade. I did Weight Watchers for many years and counted points. So, what the GLP1 does is it allows me to use the information that I already know and put it into practice. That's why these medications can be so life-changing. They don't do the work for you. They make it possible for you to finally do the work. Unfortunately, my health insurance doesn't cover any weight loss medications. So, I was faced with a choice to either pay over $1,000 a month for a name brand medication or go with a compounded version that is less expensive, which is what I did. When I started on compounded tzepide almost 2 and 1/2 years ago, it was pretty common for places to charge a smaller amount for lower doses of trespide and then increase the cost as your dose went up. I feel like now there are a lot of places that are just doing flat fee pricing and charging the same amount across the board, which might mean that you're paying more upfront than you used to. But seeing as though most people are spending more time at higher doses, it actually evens out. And I think this is probably a good thing for us. So from August 2023 when I weighed 243 lbs to October of 2024 when I reached the final weight that I have been maintaining for over a year now, 118 lb. In that 15 months, I lost 125 lbs. I spent $4,810 on my compound tepatide prescriptions. That is about $320 a month on average. On top of the actual medication, I invested myself in a lot of other ways. I have worked out with my tonal home gym from the very beginning of my weight loss journey. I've used it for strength training anywhere from 2 to 6 days a week depending on where I was in my journey. For my tonal membership, I spent about $1,000. Now, we already had the tonal, so I'm not including that in my cost breakdown here, but we did pay the monthly membership for it, which is about $65 a month. In addition to that, I did Pilates classes. I spent about $1,500 on Pilates classes. I started out doing Pilates when I was probably about 30 lbs down and feeling like I was ready to start moving my body. I really enjoyed Pilates because my body was changing in a physical way so fast and Pilates really helped me get in touch with the new body that I was experiencing every single day. Those of you who have lost 100 lbs or more can probably relate to the weird out-of- body experience you have when your body looks so different than it feels and every single day you look in the mirror and you can't believe that that's your body. You don't know what fits you and your body's just changing so fast your mind can't keep up with it. And I feel like Pilates was an amazing way for me to stay in touch with my body and to learn what it could do and stretch its limits. So I started out doing Pilates at the YMCA. The classes were like 20 or $25 a class. So I was doing those like once or twice a week. Then when I wanted to get more serious about it and start doing more Pilates, I joined Club Pilates. It was $179 a month, but I could do unlimited classes and I typically did about three classes a week. Another big cost that was completely new to me once I started this weight loss journey was spending money on protein shakes altogether. I spent about $1,200 on protein shakes. I never had a protein shake in my life before I started on a GLP1. But I knew that protein was essential and so I had to find a protein powder that I enjoyed. So while it is more expensive than protein powder that you can purchase at the store, I enjoy this protein powder. It tastes good to me and I have had a protein shake almost every day since August 2023. I use Health Code chocolate macadamia nut protein powder. It is delicious. I actually started out making my protein shake with the 30 g nutrition plan fair shakes from Costco. So, I would buy those and I would mix one scoop of chocolate macadamia nut powder from Health Code with the Fair Life Shake and some banana in there and that would be my protein shake. I probably did that for the first year until I realized that I liked the taste of it with just water. And so I quit buying the Costco shakes and I just started making with water and it's still delicious to me. But that cost really adds up because those nutrition plan shakes are like $30 at Costco. So I would buy one of those a month plus the $99 every other month I would spend on the health code protein powder. I was also spending a lot more on groceries and higher quality groceries, especially protein options. I estimate that I spent about $3,000, which is about $200 a month on these higher quality groceries. I had never thought about protein a day in my life before starting a GLP1. But I knew that I wanted to make sure that I wasn't losing muscle on this weight loss journey. My appetite was pretty suppressed right at the beginning. So, I wanted to make sure that whatever I was eating was going to be fueling me in the very best way possible. So, I was buying so much chicken and steak and turkey sausage and chicken sausage and ground beef, hard-boiled eggs, deli turkey. All of these protein options are probably the most expensive things that I spend money on at the grocery store. And I was buying a lot of them because I completely changed my eating habits where I was eating out five to seven times a week before and I started eating everything at home. I do have a family of five. We homeschool. My husband works from home so we're home all day. I'm cooking all three meals here at home. So, I definitely increased our grocery budget to accommodate for eating these healthier meals here at home. I also spent quite a bit of money on new clothes because I went down 10 pant sizes in the time I was losing weight. I went from a size 22 to a size two. I actually just did a video about how I was dressing my body during my weight loss journey and how I discovered thrifting. Before I discovered thrifting, I was spending way too much money on new clothes. And then even with thrifting, I was spending a good amount of money on new clothes to dress this body. It also became a different outlet for me where before I might turn to food, now I was turning to thrifting and dressing myself and finding ways to bring out my personality with my clothes, which I think is a much healthier habit, even though it does still cost some money. But I estimate I probably spent about $2,000 on clothes during the 15 months that I was losing weight. And then the last big expense was kind of like fitness gear and gadgets and vitamins and things. So these would be things like collagen powder. I was getting biotin and a multivitamin and then things like a little milk frother to stir up my collagen in my tea or my personal blender to mix up my protein shakes. All of those kinds of things. I estimate that I spent about $600. So altogether, the grand total, it cost me $12,610 to lose $125 lbs in 15 months. That comes out to be about $840 a month, which honestly doesn't seem that bad. Now, before I started this medication, I wanted to see what was I spending before. I had ChatGpt help me estimate based on my eating out habits. So between fast food stops by myself or with my kids, restaurants that I was going to with my family and then also alcohol here at home and out. I was spending close to $16,000 a year on habits that were draining my health and my wallet. But the real cost wasn't just financial. I am pretty young. I was 39 when I started on a GLP-1 medication. I also didn't struggle with obesity my entire life. I was what I like to call pleasantly overweight. I was maybe 5 to 20 pounds overweight for most of my adult life. But once I had three kids, turned 35, and those hormones started getting out of whack, I started putting on weight, and I was turning to food as a coping mechanism for things that were completely out of my control. I gained about 75 lbs in a single year. I tried a bunch of diets. I tried a bunch of challenges. I did intermittent fasting. I'd try to count my calories in My Fitness Pal. I did Orange Theory and tried to work out enough to burn off the extra calories I was eating. And of course, none of it worked. I lost 20 to 30 lbs several times, but immediately when I stopped doing the thing that I was doing, I gained it all back. If I could have looked 15 years into the future back then, I know what I would have seen. Type 2 diabetes, back pain, joint pain, maybe heart disease, all the coorbidities that come with carrying extra weight for far too long. My health was tanking fast and I was out of time to keep pretending that it wasn't a big deal. That's why when people talk about the cost of a GLP1, I think about what not doing anything would have cost me instead. When people say GLP1s are expensive, my honest answer is it depends on what you're comparing it to. Before I started, I was already spending that money and I wasn't getting anything in return. Now that same budget goes toward a body that can move easily through the world anywhere I want to go. Clothes that I feel confident in. Energy to be present with my kids and my family and peace around food that I never imagined was possible. It's not just paying for a medication. It's redirecting your resources toward your future self. And then there's the cost you can't see. The opportunity cost of staying stuck. Before I lost weight, I was spending so much time in bed, napping, wasting my life. I didn't have the energy to pour into the things that I loved, into my family, my friends, my hobbies, or even my business. But that wasn't the only cost. I skipped pool days with my kids. I avoided photos. I've been an avid hobby photographer since my oldest was born. And I have so many photos of my kids, of all of us together over the years, and there is a glaring 5-year gap where I don't have any photos of our family together. I said no to trips and events because I knew I wouldn't have the stamina to keep up. Travel was stressful, airplane seats were uncomfortable, and long walks sounded exhausting. When you carry extra weight, you're not just carrying around extra pounds, you're carrying limits. and I was missing out on living my life. The cost of doing nothing isn't just measured in dollars. It's measured in experiences you'll never get to have, in memories you won't get to make, and years you can't ever get back. And gosh, my life has done a complete 180. Life now is so different than life was 2 and 1/2 years ago. Yes, I look different. I've lost 125 lbs. But what matters more than that is I have energy. I wake up every day refreshed. I can bound out of bed, ready to tackle whatever the day will bring. I get up and I walk every morning. I soak in the sunshine, say hi to my neighbors, and enjoy the beautiful world outside. I homeschool my three kids and I'm fully present with them and enjoying the time we have learning together. I love spending time with my husband and friends. I have hobbies like pickle ball and creative pursuits like knitting and reading. And I've built this YouTube channel that's turning into a little side business, something that never could have happened before. The medication didn't just change my body. It gave me back my time, my confidence, and my ability to show up in life for what I actually want. So, if you're trying to decide if a GLP1 is worth it, here's what I'd suggest. Look honestly at your spending. Think about what you'll gain, not just what you'll lose. And plan for sustainability. Because this isn't just about weight loss. It's about building a life you love enough to maintain. So yes, GLP1s are an investment financially and emotionally, but so is staying stuck. So, was it worth it to spend over $12,000 to lose $125 and change my life? You bet it was. If this helped you see the numbers and maybe yourself a little differently, I would love it if you would like this video, subscribe to my channel, and maybe share this video with a friend who's thinking about whether a GLP1 is worth it for them. Thanks so much for watching.

- I Stopped Ozempic and Regained 50 Pounds - Don't Make My Mistake: Kind: captions Language: en Imagine losing 50 lbs in just 6 months. You feel amazing. Your clothes fit better. Your confidence is soaring. Then you stop taking OMPic. And within weeks, the weight starts creeping back. Sound familiar? You're not alone. And today, we're diving deep into why this happens and what you can actually do about it. If you're watching this video because you're worried about weight regain or you're considering us Empic, make sure to hit that like button and subscribe to Core Health. We share health tips, fasting guides, nutrition advice, and fitness strategies every single day to help you stay healthy for life. Hi everyone, welcome back to Core Health. I'm so glad you're here today because we're tackling one of the most talked about topics in weight loss right now, OMIC weight regain. You've probably heard the success stories. Maybe you've even experienced the dramatic weight loss yourself. But here's what many doctors and influencers aren't talking about enough. What happens when you stop taking the medication? The truth is weight regain after stopping oenic is incredibly common. Some studies suggest that people can regain up to 2/3 of the weight they lost within just one year of stopping the medication. That's a really big deal, especially if you've invested time, money, and hope into this treatment. So let's break down exactly why this happens and more importantly what you can do to protect your progress. First let's understand what ompic actually does in your body. Zmpic is a medication that was originally designed for type 2 diabetes but it became famous for weight loss. The active ingredient is called semaglutide. It works by mimicking a hormone in your body called GLP1. This hormone does several important things. It slows down how fast food leaves your stomach, making you feel full for longer. It signals to your brain that you're satisfied and don't need more food, and it helps regulate your blood sugar levels. When you're taking Ozeic, your appetite decreases significantly. Many people report that they simply don't think about food as much. The constant cravings disappear. The urge to snack at night goes away. Food noise, as some people call it, gets turned down to almost zero. This makes it much easier to eat less and lose weight. But here's the catch. These effects only last as long as you're taking the medication. Now, let's talk about why weight regain happens when you stop. There are several biological and behavioral reasons, and understanding them is the first step to preventing regain. Reason number one is that your appetite comes roaring back. Remember how ampic quiets your hunger signals? Well, when you stop taking it, those signals return. and sometimes they come back even stronger than before. Your body has been in a calorie deficit, which means you've been eating less than your body burns. Your body interprets this as a threat. It thinks you're starving. So, when the medication is gone, your hunger hormones like ghrein go into overdrive, trying to get you to eat more and restore the weight you lost. Reason number two is metabolic adaptation. When you lose weight, your metabolism slows down. This is a natural survival mechanism. Your body becomes more efficient at using energy, which means you burn fewer calories than you did before, even at the same weight. So, if you go back to eating the same amount of food you ate before ompic, you'll likely gain weight because your body now needs fewer calories than it used to. Reason number three, and this is a big one, is that many people don't build sustainable habits while on oz. When the medication is doing all the heavy lifting by suppressing your appetite, it's easy to skip the hard work of learning portion control, understanding nutrition, managing stress eating, and building a consistent exercise routine. Then, when the medication stops, you're left without the tools you need to maintain your weight loss. Reason number four is psychological factors. Weight loss can be an emotional journey. Some people use food for comfort, stress relief, or celebration. If these underlying patterns aren't addressed while you're losing weight, they'll come back when the medications stop. Food is not just fuel. It's connected to our emotions, our social lives, and our daily routines. Without addressing these connections, long-term success is really difficult. If you're finding this information helpful, please take a second to subscribe to Core Health and hit that notification bell. We're building a community of people who want real honest health information without the hype. Let's continue. So, now that we understand why weight regains, let's talk about solutions. What can you actually do to keep the weight off? Solution number one is to use your time on ozenic wisely. Think of ompic as a tool, not a magic cure. while you're taking it and your appetite is reduced. This is your golden opportunity to build healthy habits that will last. Practice portion control. Use smaller plates. Learn to recognize true hunger versus boredom or emotional eating. Learn about nutrition. Understand what foods keep you full and satisfied. Focus on protein, fiber, and healthy fats. These nutrients help you stay satisfied longer. Start an exercise routine that you actually enjoy. It doesn't have to be intense. Walking, swimming, dancing, and yoga and something you'll stick with because you like it, not because you're forcing yourself. Solution number two is to not stop ompic suddenly. Work with your doctor to create a plan. Some doctors recommend slowly reducing your dose rather than stopping completely all at once. This gives your body time to adjust. Others might suggest staying on a maintenance dose long term. The key is having a medical professional guide you through this transition. Everyone's situation is different. Solution number three is focus on protein. This is huge for weight maintenance. Protein helps preserve your muscle mass, which keeps your metabolism higher. It also keeps you feeling full and satisfied. Aim to include a good source of protein at every meal. Think eggs, chicken, fish, Greek yogurt, beans, lentils, tofu. Protein should be a priority on your plate. Solution number four is manage your stress. Stress is one of the biggest triggers for overeating and weight regain. When you're stressed, your body releases cortisol, which can increase appetite and cravings, especially for sugary and fatty foods. Find healthy ways to manage stress. This might be meditation, deep breathing exercises, journaling, talking to a friend, or spending time in nature. Stress management is just as important as diet and exercise. Solution number five is get enough sleep. Poor sleep messes with your hunger hormones. When you don't sleep enough, ghrelin, your hunger hormone, goes up and leptin, your fullness hormone, goes down. This makes you hungrier and less satisfied after eating. Aim for 7 to n hours of quality sleep each night. Create a bedtime routine. Keep your bedroom cool and dark. Limit screen time before bed. Solution number six is practice mindful eating. This means paying attention to what you're eating, how much you're eating, and why you're eating. Eat slowly. Put your fork down between bites. Check in with yourself. Am I actually hungry, or am I eating because I'm bored, sad, or stressed? Stop eating when you're satisfied, not when you're stuffed. These simple practices can make a massive difference. Solution number seven is build a support system. Tell your friends and family about your goals. Join a support group either online or in person. Consider working with a nutritionist or therapist who specializes in weight management. You don't have to do this alone. Having people who understand your journey and can encourage you makes everything easier. Solution number eight is track your progress, but don't obsess. Weigh yourself once a week, not every day. Your weight naturally fluctuates from day to day because of water retention, hormones, and digestion. Weekly weigh-ins give you a better picture of the trend without the daily stress. You can also track other things like how your clothes fit, your energy levels, and how you feel overall. Solution number nine is be prepared for setbacks. Life happens. There will be birthday parties, holidays, stressful weeks at work, and vacations. You might overeat sometimes. That's completely normal and human. The key is not to let one bad day turn into a bad week or a bad month. Get back on track with your very next meal. No guilt, no shame. Just move forward. Solution number 10 is understand your hunger cues. Again, when you're on ompic, you might forget what natural hunger feels like. As the medication leaves your system, you need to relearn the difference between physical hunger and head hunger. Physical hunger builds gradually, becomes with physical signs like a growling stomach, low energy, or difficulty concentrating. Head hunger comes on suddenly. It's usually triggered by emotions, seeing food, or specific times of day. Learning to recognize the difference helps you respond appropriately. Are you really hungry or are you just bored, tired, or seeking comfort? Solution number 11 is create a structured eating schedule. Going too long without eating can lead to extreme hunger and overeating. Try eating every 3 to four hours. This keeps your blood sugar stable and prevents you from getting so hungry that you lose control. Structure doesn't mean rigidity. It just means having a general plan for when you'll eat meals and snacks. This takes away a lot of decision-making and reduces the chances of impulsive eating. Solution number 12 is keep tempting foods out of your immediate environment. This isn't about deprivation. It's about making healthy choices easier. If there's a box of cookies on your counter, you'll think about those cookies all day. If they're not there, you won't. Stock your kitchen with foods that support your goals. Keep cut vegetables ready to eat. Have healthy snacks portioned out. Make it convenient to eat well. When you do want to enjoy treats, you can go out and get them mindfully rather than mindlessly snacking at home. Before we wrap up, I want you to remember to like this video and subscribe to Core Health for more content like this. We're here to support you with daily health tips, nutrition advice, fasting guides, and fitness strategies that actually work in real life. Let's talk about the bigger picture for a moment. The truth about OMIC and weight regain isn't that the medication doesn't work. It does work and it's helped many people lose significant weight. The issue is that weight loss is only half the battle. Keeping it off requires a complete shift in your lifestyle, your habits, and your relationship with food. No medication can do that for you. Only you can do that work. One thing that's really important to understand is that your body will fight to regain weight. This isn't a character flaw or lack of willpower. It's biology. Your body has a set point weight range that it tries to defend. When you lose a significant amount of weight, your body thinks something is wrong. It increases hunger hormones. It decreases hormones that make you feel full. It slows down your metabolism. It even makes food look more appealing and rewarding in your brain. This is why maintaining weight loss is often harder than losing weight in the first place. Knowing this helps you prepare mentally and strategically. Another important aspect is the social side of eating. Food is a huge part of how we connect with others. Family dinners, office celebrations, dates, girls nights out. When you're trying to maintain your weight, you need strategies for these situations. You can eat before social events so you're not starving when you arrive. You can offer to bring a healthy dish to share. You can focus on the social connection rather than just the food. You can practice saying no thank you without overexplaining or feeling guilty. Having a plan for social situations reduces anxiety and helps you stay on track without isolating yourself from the people you care about. Let's also talk about the financial aspect. All is expensive. Many people can't afford to stay on it long term, especially if insurance doesn't cover it for weight loss. This is another reason why building sustainable habits is so critical. You need to be able to maintain your weight loss without depending on expensive medication. The skills you build, the habits you create, and the knowledge you gain are free, and they're yours forever. If you're currently taking ampic, use this time to really invest in yourself. Learn about nutrition, build an exercise habit, work on your mental health, address your emotional eating pattern. These are the things that will serve you for the rest of your life with or without medication. If you've already stopped taking ampic and you're experiencing weight regain, please don't be discouraged. It's not too late. You can turn things around. Start implementing the strategies we talked about today. Focus on protein. Manage your stress. Get good sleep. Practice mindful eating. Build your support system. Small, consistent actions add up to big results over time. And if you're thinking about starting OMIC, go into it with your eyes wide open. Have a conversation with your doctor about what happens after you stop. Make a plan for long-term maintenance before you even start losing weight. Set yourself up for lasting success, not just quick results. The bottom line is this. OMIC can be an effective tool for weight loss. But it's just that, a tool. The real power to maintain your health and your weight comes from the daily choices you make, the habits you build, and the care you give yourself. Your body is amazing. It's capable of incredible things. Treat it with respect, nourish it well, move it regularly, and give it the rest it needs. Thank you so much for watching today.
